Oracle just let go of up to 30,000 employees. Despite one of its best quarters in years. 12,000 of them were in India.

We've seen similar waves from Meta, Amazon, Microsoft, Google. The pattern is the same: record profits, and layoffs in the same quarter.

Here's something I've noticed. You know where this isn't happening? In manufacturing, in supply chain, in logistics, in packaging, in warehousing.

These aren't the industries people talk about at parties. They don't make for great LinkedIn bios. But they're also not firing thousands of people while posting record earnings.

There's a massive concentration of tech and AI talent in companies that are already tech-first. And the result of that concentration is exactly this: the more efficient these companies get, the fewer people they need.

Meanwhile, industries like manufacturing are practically greenfield. A factory doing Rs. 50 crore in revenue might still run procurement on WhatsApp. A logistics operator might not have a single dashboard. A packaging company might have no idea what their real unit economics look like.

That's not a problem, that's an opportunity.

If you're a tech professional updating your CV today, consider reaching out to a manufacturer, a warehouse operator, a trucking company. The speed at which you can grow in these spaces is unlike anything in tech. You could even buy one of these businesses and completely transform it.

It won't sound exciting at the next dinner party. But the upside is real, and almost nobody is competing for it.

Piyush Tiwari, SACHIN JAIN, Hitesh Lunawat, Raxit Bisht, Abhishek Kumar Singh, Aman P., Ankur Kandoi, Sanjana Kapoor, Abhishek Rana, krishnananda, Ashutosh Dhar | 124 | 1 | 1
In every manufacturing plant I've visited, there's one team that gets blamed for everything.

- Production stops. Procurement didn't order on time.
- Costs are high. Procurement should have negotiated better.
- Supplier delivers late. Procurement didn't vet them properly.
- Quality fails. Procurement chose the wrong vendor.

It's always procurement's fault.

I saw this at BHEL. I see it now across 75+ enterprise deployments at ProcMart. The pattern never changes. But here's what nobody says out loud.
- Procurement can't fix a finance team that delays supplier payments by 60 days and then expects vendors to prioritize your orders.
It can't fix engineering that changes specifications after the purchase order is raised.
It can't fix leadership that quietly tolerates maverick buying because "production can't stop."
It can't fix an approval workflow that takes 14 days for a ₹5,000 purchase.

Yet when any of those break, the breakdown shows up in procurement first.

Procurement sits at the intersection of every other function: finance, operations, suppliers, compliance, leadership. When anything goes wrong anywhere, it flows through procurement before it becomes visible.

So procurement gets blamed for problems it didn't create and can't solve alone.

The real issue is never the procurement team. It's that procurement has no single owner above the factory floor who wins when it works and loses when it doesn't.

Until that changes, nothing changes. The same team gets blamed. The same problems repeat and the procurements team would get burned out.

Piyush Tiwari, SACHIN JAIN, Hitesh Lunawat, Raxit Bisht, Abhishek Kumar Singh, Aman P., Ankur Kandoi, Sanjana Kapoor, Abhishek Rana, krishnananda, Ashutosh Dhar | 109 | 7 | 3
I started my career at BHEL, a government PSU. Now I run a tech startup that helps manufacturers fix their procurement operations.

The decade in between taught me one thing I wish I had known at the start: technology adoption in manufacturing almost never fails because of the technology.

At BHEL, I watched ERP rollouts struggle. The software was solid. The implementation teams were competent. But the factory floor kept doing things the old way. WhatsApp approvals, maverick buying, workarounds that became "how we do things." Nobody had told the procurement manager on the floor why this was his priority. Nobody had agreed on what success even looked like.

Then I built ProcMart and hit the exact same wall from the other side.

75+ enterprise deployments later, the pattern is identical every time. The symptoms look like technology problems. They almost never are.

- "The software isn't being used." Nobody defined what adoption actually means at the floor level.
- "The factory floor resists." Leadership never made it their priority, just their initiative.
- "We need better software." The real problem is behavioral, not functional.
- "ROI isn't showing." Nobody agreed on how to measure it before go-live.

Technology adoption is not a deployment problem. It's a definition and ownership problem. Until someone in leadership personally wins when it succeeds, the factory floor will find a way to survive around it.

The software is rarely the reason it fails. The missing owner almost always is. | 127 | 1 | 1
Last week I spoke to a candidate who had quit his job without another offer in hand.

He had spent his career in startups. Then he made a deliberate move to a legacy company because they wanted him to lead their AI transformation. He was excited. Real scale, real operations, real impact.

Six months in, he realized AI transformation wasn't actually their priority. It was on the roadmap. It had a budget. But when it competed for leadership attention against quarterly targets, it lost. Every time.

So he quit. Without a backup plan.

I've seen this play out too many times. And honestly, we learned this the hard way at ProcMart.

When we started, we believed that if we built a genuinely useful product, adoption would follow. It didn't. We'd get the contract signed at HQ, then hit a wall at the factory floor. The procurement manager had his own system. His team had their own habits. Nobody had told them this was a priority.

The product wasn't the problem. The buy-in was.

We eventually figured out that two things have to be true for any transformation to actually land. Someone in the top leadership has to own it. And someone in that leadership has to personally win when it succeeds.

If neither is true, the initiative gets polite support and quiet resistance. It never dies officially. It just never really lives either.

That candidate wasn't the problem. He walked into a transformation that leadership had commissioned but never actually prioritized. That's a different thing.

Before taking any transformation role, ask one question: who in the leadership team loses sleep if this fails?

If there's no clear answer, you already know how the story ends. | 157 | 13 | 0
I grew up around an ecosystem most policy documents call a "legacy industrial cluster."

In Bhilai, the steel plant created the cluster. The cluster created everything else - MSMEs, fabricators, packaging companies, logistics operators. The whole city ran on it. Thousands of families found their footing not because of the plant directly, but because of what grew around it.

Over time, many of these ecosystems stopped compounding. Not because the manufacturing stopped, but because the infrastructure around it didn't evolve - old procurement systems, disconnected suppliers, talent that went to Bangalore instead of staying to build.

The Budget this year announced a scheme to revive 200 such clusters - upgrading infrastructure and technology to make them cost-competitive again. It didn't make headlines the way the semiconductor mission did. But for anyone who grew up in places like Nagpur, Surat, Ludhiana, or Bhilai, it's the line that matters most.

Reviving them isn't a romantic project. It's an economic one. For the same GDP contribution, manufacturing creates far more jobs than services. And most of those jobs live in cities that don't make startup Twitter.

The scheme matters. But what matters more is someone actually showing up on the factory floor to do the work.

That part doesn't have a budget line. It just needs people willing to do it. | 77 | 0 | 0
The government is putting ₹12.2 lakh crore behind manufacturing this year.

That's a real signal. The kind that changes investment theses, hiring decisions, and where founders choose to build.

But here's something I keep thinking about: every rupee of capex only compounds if the operational layer underneath it works. Roads need suppliers. Factories need procurement. Plants need the invisible infrastructure that nobody photographs.

A few years ago, I visited a bio-fuel plant in Aurangabad. The facility existed, the feedstock existed, the demand existed, but the sourcing was fragmented, cash flow was a mess, and nobody owned the supply chain end to end. The plant was running at a fraction of its potential.

More capital wouldn't have fixed that. Someone had to show up and fix the operations.

India's manufacturing ambition has always been bigger than its infrastructure reality. For the first time, the infrastructure is catching up - ₹12.2 lakh crore in capex, semiconductor missions. legacy cluster revival, biofuel policy, container manufacturing schemes.

The hardware is being built.

The question now is whether the software, the procurement, the supplier networks, the operational discipline, keeps pace.

The factories are coming. The supply chains need to be ready for them.

That's where the real work is. | 109 | 0 | 5
Last year I met a founder who had raised a large seed round about a year ago.

He should have been energized. Instead, he told me he wanted to quit and build something bootstrapped. Not because he was tired of the work. He was tired of the race: the pitch cycles, the investor updates, the pressure to show metrics that justified the next round before he'd figured out if the current one was working.

That conversation stayed with me.

I've come to believe that capital is a multiplier. But a multiplier only works if there's something real underneath it. Multiply zero and you get zero. Multiply a broken unit economics model and you get a bigger broken model, faster.

When we started ProcMart, we made a deliberate choice not to raise external capital until we knew what we were trying to multiply. For five years, we fixed the fundamentals. Built the supplier network. Figured out where we actually made money. It was slow and unglamorous, and I watched peers raise large rounds and move faster on paper.

But raising capital before you have something worth multiplying is like building large walls around an empty plot. The fort looks impressive. There's nothing inside worth protecting.

When we finally raised, we knew exactly which levers to pull. The capital did what capital is supposed to do: it accelerated something that was already working.

The founder I met last year wasn't weak. He had just spent two years building walls. Nobody had asked him what was inside. | 143 | 6 | 3
You compete for market share when you can't figure out how to grow the market.

If your success requires taking customers from others, you've already admitted you can't create new value. Early at ProcMart, I realized we weren’t competing with other platforms at all. We were competing with the barriers that kept 50,000 manufacturers out of organized procurement.

Those manufacturers weren't choosing between us and competitors. They were choosing jugaad, WhatsApp approvals, Excel sheets, maverick buying, because organized procurement felt impossible.

Markets look fixed because incumbents build for people who already have access, budgets, and literacy. That creates the illusion of zero-sum competition. Every "market size" is actually a measure of "how many people can access the solution with current tools." But when you eliminate the barriers that artificially constrain markets, the market explodes.

Shopify didn't compete for the 40,000 existing online stores. They made it possible for millions who couldn't afford stores to create them. The market wasn't 40,000 stores. It was everyone who wanted to sell something but couldn't.

The history of technology is the history of abundance replacing scarcity. Online retail didn't take market share from malls. It created entirely new purchasing behavior. Every major company grew the pie, they didn't fight over it.

So the real question isn’t, “How do we beat competitors?”
It’s this, "What prevents non-customers from becoming customers, and how do we eliminate it?"

Answer that, and competition becomes noise. Ignore it, and you'll spend your career fighting over scraps while someone else feeds the world. | 83 | 2 | 2
The biggest mistake I see companies make is hiring "strategic thinkers" who are too senior to get their hands dirty.

When I hire leaders at ProcMart, I look for one thing above everything else. And it's not pedigree, years of experience, or even domain expertise. I look for people who can't stay away from the details

Leaders who want to be in deployment calls. Who visit factories without being asked. Who review implementation docs because they care about getting it right.

In B2B manufacturing, strategic thinking siloed from operational reality is useless. You can't build a good strategy if you don't understand what's actually happening on factory floors.

I'd rather hire someone junior who obsesses over details than someone senior who delegates everything and reviews dashboards. Because excellence lives in details. And you can't maintain excellence if you're not in the details yourself.

If you're building a B2B company, hire people who care enough to stay operationally involved. Everything else can be taught.

Piyush Tiwari, SACHIN JAIN, Hitesh Lunawat, Raxit Bisht, Abhishek Kumar Singh, Aman P., Ankur Kandoi, Sanjana Kapoor, Abhishek Rana, krishnananda, Ashutosh Dhar | 153 | 7 | 5
If your product needs extensive training, you’ve failed at design.

Complexity in the problem does not require complexity in the interface. I’m not against training, but users shouldn’t need it just to complete basic tasks.

When a factory manager tells me, “I’m not technical enough for this,” that’s my failure as a builder. Not his limitation as a user. He should be able to create his first purchase order in five minute, without training, without documentation, and without calling support.

One of the biggest mistakes organizations make when implementing new technology is assuming employees will intuitively understand how the system works.

We work with 75+ large enterprises and our platform handles multi-location procurement, complex approval workflows, thousands of suppliers, and compliance tracking. It's complex, but it lives on the backend. The procurement manager on the factory floor just sees a clean interface that matches how he already thinks about buying.

The complexity is our job to handle. Not his job to comprehend.

Every time someone says, “users need to learn how enterprise systems work,” I translate, “We couldn’t figure out how to make it intuitive.”

The goal isn’t to make users smarter about your software. The goal is to make your software smarter about your users.

Piyush Tiwari, SACHIN JAIN, Hitesh Lunawat, Raxit Bisht, Abhishek Kumar Singh, Aman P., Ankur Kandoi, Sanjana Kapoor, Abhishek Rana, krishnananda, Ashutosh Dhar | 88 | 2 | 3
I used to think we were building a procurement marketplace. I was wrong.

Last year, we met with one of India’s largest conglomerates. They could easily get better rates than us on packaging and MRO. In some categories, they already do.

But they still chose ProcMart.

Not for cheaper procurement, but for our ability to understand their workflows deeply. To solutionize faster. To define approval processes their teams could deploy without reinventing the wheel.

Over 10 years and 75+ enterprise deployments, we’ve accumulated intelligence that can’t be replicated overnight. We understand where procurement breaks. We know which approval structures actually work. We’ve seen what fails.

That accumulated knowledge is our moat. Not our supplier network. Not our tech stack. Our intelligence from implementations.

Every deployment teaches us something new. Every failed workflow becomes a recognizable pattern. Every successful rollout adds to our playbook.

India’s manufacturing is expected to grow from 14% to 21% of GDP by 2032. As this happens, manufacturers won’t want to be the first to figure things out. They’ll want partners who’ve already lived their problems.

Your pricing can be matched. Your features can be copied. But the intelligence you build by solving real problems for real customers, that compounds.

Piyush Tiwari, SACHIN JAIN, Hitesh Lunawat, Raxit Bisht, Abhishek Kumar Singh, Aman P., Ankur Kandoi, Sanjana Kapoor, Abhishek Rana, krishnananda, Ashutosh Dhar | 235 | 0 | 3
Studying competition is the fastest way to irrelevance.

It doesn't make you faster, it makes you a derivative. While you're analyzing their race line, they're analyzing yours, and you're both losing time to whoever's staring at the track itself.

Sure, you need to have market intelligence, but it comes in two forms:
1. Customer intelligence (proactive & keeps compounding)
2. Competitor intelligence (reactive & makes you a derivative)

We made a choice early on in ProcMart, every dollar that could go to competitive analysis would go to understanding customers instead.

For this reason, I don't believe in battle cards, you don't need feature comparisons, you need to have customer problem maps. You need to document every customer crisis you have solved, everytime you've failed (and won). That becomes your deployment playbook and your roadmap.

Last month, a customer told us they chose ProcMart because we defined their problem better than anyone else, and could predict what would break before it did.

That's why I keep saying, customer obsession isn't a marketing slogan. It's an operational choice about where you invest your time and attention.

I'd rather have deep knowledge of 50 customer problems than surface knowledge of 500 competitor features. Because the former builds moat, the latter just keeps you busy.

Piyush Tiwari, SACHIN JAIN, Hitesh Lunawat, Raxit Bisht, Abhishek Kumar Singh, Aman P., Ankur Kandoi, Sanjana Kapoor, Abhishek Rana, krishnananda, Ashutosh Dhar, Anshu (Sudhanshu) Srivastav | 239 | 4 | 3
Someone asked me recently, “How do you find your passion?”

I don’t think you find it. I think it finds you. When I started ProcMart, I wasn’t chasing a trend. I wasn’t looking at market opportunity slides. I was solving a problem I had lived.

During my BHEL days, I saw procurement chaos every single day. MRO grinding plants to a halt, maverick buying, and no accountability. I hated it at the time.

But that frustration, that “this shouldn’t be this hard” feeling, became my conviction. I couldn’t have manufactured that. No amount of market research would have given me that insight.

Here’s what I believe now. You can’t force product market fit in yourself either. If you’re building something because it looks exciting, or because investors are interested, or because everyone else is doing it, you’ll give up the moment it gets hard.

But if you’re building something because you can’t not build it, because you’ve seen the problem up close and it bothers you every day, you won’t stop.

That’s not passion. That’s obsession born from lived experience. And you don’t need motivation when you’ve actually lived the problem. You need solutions.

So if you’re looking for your passion, stop looking. Go live in the messiest, most chaotic version of the industry you’re curious about. Your passion will find you in that chaos. | 113 | 2 | 2
A prospect asked us last month, "Why shouldn't we just go directly to your suppliers?"

My instinct was to defend. To list our value adds. To talk about our platform, our tech, our network. I didn't.

Instead, I said, "You absolutely can. Let me walk you through what will happen." I took him through it. Step by step.

First, you'll need to identify which suppliers are reliable. You'll call 50, maybe 5 will respond. Then you'll negotiate terms. They'll agree to 15 days, deliver in 30. You'll have no recourse because there's no contract accountability. Meanwhile, your line stops. Fingers get pointed. You firefight.

By the end, he said, "I see why this doesn't work."

I didn't sell him ProcMart. He sold himself.

Here's what I learned from someone much wiser than me. When you spoon-feed, you insult your audience. When you let them discover, they feel smart. And when they feel smart, they trust you.

This applies everywhere. In sales, in team building and in manufacturing. If you're explaining every single detail, you're not respecting their intelligence. You're treating them like they can't figure it out.

The best founders, the best salespeople, the best manufacturers don't fill in all the blanks. They create space for realization. Because people don't buy what you tell them. They buy what they discover for themselves. | 165 | 5 | 3
ProcMart turns 10 this week.

I’m writing this not to talk about our growth, profits, or valuation, but to reflect on how these ten years have shaped us.

Looking back, I don’t think individual brilliance or some maverick move got us here. What actually did was our stubborn refusal to give up. Quarter after quarter, year after year, we just kept showing up. There were no dramatic turning points, just small, steady improvements. But those small improvements opened new geographies, new categories, funding, revenue… everything.

One thing I’ve come to believe is the importance of being flexible with the approach, but unshakeable about the problem you're solving. A customer told me a few months ago, “You’ve been saying the same thing for the last eight years.” And I said, “That's the point.” When things don’t work, it’s easy to change the goal to something simpler or safer. But most of the time, it’s not the goal that’s wrong, it’s the way we’re trying to get there.

This mindset now defines us. Before stepping away from a category, geography, or even a person, we ask, “Are we just approaching this the wrong way? What else can we try?”

As we hit the 10 year mark, I am grateful to my customers who trusted us early, to my team who stood by me through everything, and to our investors who believed this problem was worth solving.

There’s no big finish here, just a quiet acknowledgment of how far we’ve come, and confidence that the next decade will demand the same grit, patience, and willingness to try again.

Piyush Tiwari, SACHIN JAIN, Hitesh Lunawat, Raxit Bisht, Abhishek Kumar Singh, Aman P., Ankur Kandoi, Sanjana Kapoor, Abhishek Rana, krishnananda, Ashutosh Dhar | 428 | 44 | 9
Ten years into building ProcMart, I've noticed something about profitability that nobody talks about.

It's not just a financial milestone. It's a clarity filter.

When I talk to founders, I hear justifications for losses: "We're building infrastructure." "Network effects take time." Both can be true. But they're also convenient.

Profitability forces a different question: Is what we're doing actually creating value that customers will pay for?

That question kills features that sound clever but serve two customers. Expansion ideas that look like growth but have no path to unit economics. Investments that boost activity but not contribution.

What survives gets sharper. Supplier onboarding that reduces friction. Automation that makes service better and costs lower. Focus on categories where you've built real depth.

I've also seen this change who joins companies. The best builders don't ask about funding rounds anymore. They ask about margins, about what customers value, about whether the business can sustain itself.

Because profit isn't anti-growth. It's anti-confusion.

Capital accelerates momentum. But profitability is what proves you have momentum worth accelerating. | 166 | 5 | 1
A few years ago, I met an MSME operator who'd been running his business for 18 years.

Single product line. Steady revenue around ₹40 crore. When I asked why he never diversified, he said, "Each new thing is a 30% tax on the original thing."

I didn't fully understand it then. But I do now.

At ProcMart, we get asked often if we solve for direct procurement too. It's tempting, quick wins, bigger deals, top-line boost.

But I've noticed that every time we've chased opportunities outside indirect procurement, our core business slows down. Not dramatically, but enough to matter. Because focus isn't just about what you do. It's about protecting what you're building from dilution.

That operator was right. When you add something new, it doesn't just require resources, it taxes everything you've already built. Your team's attention. Your supplier relationships. Your clarity about who you serve.

Now when we expand into new categories, I try to do some of the heavy lifting myself before it becomes someone's full-time role. They get the leverage of everything we've built, our suppliers, our systems, our distribution, but they don't drain mindspace from our cash-generating businesses.

Their blast radius stays small. What's working keeps working.

Saying no is hard, especially when the opportunity looks good. But staying focused lets your work compound in one direction instead of fragmenting across three.

Might sound old school. But it works. | 160 | 5 | 4
In high-trust B2B, your story bank is your only moat.

Features are commoditized in 18 months. Stories compound for decades.

In 2019, we lost a deal to a competitor. It hurt. Later, that same procurement head called us back. Their vendor had perfect uptime but zero empathy during a crisis.

He said: "You told me about the bearing that failed at your customer's plant and how your team drove 4 hours to deliver a replacement at midnight. I need a partner who's lived my chaos, not just studied it."

That's the power of stories built from lived experiences.

Every failure you survived. Every emergency you solved. Every customer who called you first when things broke. These become un-replicable because they required time and pain.

Competitors can copy your features in months. They can match your pricing. They can hire your engineers. But they can't copy 9 years of 2 AM phone calls answered. They can't simulate the credibility of showing up for your customers.

This is the arbitrage nobody talks about: while everyone optimizes their pitch deck, the real compounding happens in the stories only time can write.

Your positioning isn't what you claim. It's what you've survived. | 111 | 3 | 3
Our biggest competitor isn't another SaaS platform.

It's the Excel sheet the procurement manager has maintained for 7 years.

He knows every formula. Every workaround. Every vendor's personal number is in there. It's ugly, broken, and irreplaceable, because it's his.

When we pitch ProcMart, we're not competing on features. We're competing against his identity.

That Excel sheet represents his expertise. Moving to our platform means admitting: "My system doesn't work." For a 20-year veteran, that's not a product decision, it's an identity crisis.

I learned this when a plant head rejected us despite his CEO approving budget. He said: "Your software might be better. But my team knows my way."

So we changed the pitch. Instead of replacing his Excel, we positioned as building on top of what he'd already created. Import his sheet. Keep his logic. Add our automation as a layer that makes his system more powerful.

Adoption jumped 3x.

Here's the insight: People don't resist change, they resist loss. Loss of control. Loss of credit. Loss of the identity they've built.

Every market has an invisible incumbent. It's not always a competitor. Sometimes it's just the customer's existing solution to their own problem.

Beat the product, but honour the person who built it.

Piyush Tiwari, SACHIN JAIN, Hitesh Lunawat, Raxit Bisht, Abhishek Kumar Singh, Aman P., Ankur Kandoi, Sanjana Kapoor, Abhishek Rana, krishnananda, Ashutosh Dhar | 225 | 8 | 5
When I joined BHEL, they hadn't hired freshers for a few years before our batch.

That created what I'd call an experience arbitrage. Suddenly, people in their early twenties were sitting in front of senior leaders, customers, and vendors, making decisions, facing heat, solving problems we had no textbooks for.

I hated it at the time - long hours, real pressure. Not what you expect from government jobs.

But looking back, that accelerated my learning by at least a decade.

It taught me something I carry to this day: coachability matters more than experience.

You don't just bet on someone who's at the peak of their career. You bet when they're on the slope, when they're learning fast, adapting quickly, and hungry to grow.

Even now at ProcMart, some of our early team members surprise me with how quickly they pick up new problems and solve them. It's become muscle memory for them. They have learned to find answers fast.

The problems we're solving in manufacturing procurement don't have textbooks yet. We're writing the playbook as we go.

Smart and hardworking are not differentiator, you can always find smart and hard-working people, and with AI, the experience is getting commoditised.

But being coachable is what lets someone grow with the company instead of plateauing. | 201 | 10 | 6
“We will figure margins at scale.”

I hear versions of this often. But I believe, if something is broken, scale amplifies it, it does not fix it. And this applies to broken unit economics as well.

When we decided to step into manufacturing, the first spreadsheet we signed was not a revenue projection, it was the unit economics.

Input costs with realistic variance. Scrap rates at the line, not the brochure. Run‑rate payroll that includes the night shift nobody accounts for. Working capital that assumes a customer will delay payment exactly when you need it most.

Margin‑less growth looks exciting on LinkedIn and press releases, but it looks terrifying on the factory floor. The day you cannot pay for resin or paper because a big invoice is late, is the day strategy meets reality.

Your team deserves better, your customers do too.

If a unit cannot make money, a thousand units will just lose money faster. | 120 | 7 | 3
I keep coming across manufacturing plants handling procurements with "jugaad."

It’s WhatsApp approvals at midnight, it’s a vendor’s man-on-the-ground rushing to source material, it’s someone going rogue with maverick buying because “production can’t stop.”

The reality is, no amount of software training will fix this.

You can deploy the best tools. You can integrate every supplier, automate POs, and standardize catalogs. But none of that matters if there isn’t one accountable layer owning the indirect procurement process. Without ownership, the software becomes just another reporting tool, not a control mechanism.

The deeper truth is: indirect procurement isn't a software problem; it's a behavioural, operational, cash-flow, trust and accountability problem.

What it really needs is a connective tissue between IT, Finance, Procurement, Suppliers, and even Financiers. That connective tissue brings accountability to indirect buying. It ensures that the deployment doesn't remain a training exercise, it also results in cost savings, improved efficiency.

That connective tissue is ProcMart, helping manufacturers look beyond the "jugaad." | 135 | 2 | 4
I’ve raised over $40M in funding, and I’ve also failed to raise when I thought I had everything right.

The difference is almost always clarity.

Investors don’t live your problem every day. They decide fast. If your pitch doesn’t clearly answer three things in plain English, what problem you solve, who you solve it for, and why it matters now, you lose them.

This is where most founders slip (including me in the early days), they focus on product, not the problem. They talk about features, not outcomes. They stack logos and vision slides, and leave their strongest customer stories buried in the end.

You can be building a brilliant business, but still telling your story wrong.

Fundraising is not that different from enterprise sales. The best pitches I’ve seen start with one operational win that matters to the buyer, repeat it on the last slide, and back it up with retention and frequency of use.

Sure, the story doesn't replace execution, it shows that you have execution. If you can’t tell the story and value in 60 seconds, you’re not ready to raise. | 319 | 9 | 3
I grew up in a small city, Bhilai.

It’s known as the Steel City of Central India because of the Bhilai Steel Plant, where my father worked. I grew up in the staff quarters, surrounded by a community shaped by industry.

Thanks to the steel plant, Bhilai became home to thousands of MSMEs, small manufacturers, logistics companies, packaging, fabricators, and more.

What always amazed me was how instinctively these business owners manage credit. Ten-day, fifteen-day, thirty-day credit lines, all negotiated, honored, and optimized, with the cost of credit blended into every transaction, every quote, every decision.

No one teaches them, it’s in their bloodstream. That’s how they scale.

I’ve always believed tech founders should treat tech debt the same way, as a credit line.

If an engineering problem can be solved by our 100th engineer, I prefer leaving it for them, as long as I can afford the 'daily interest' of semi-automated or manual workflows in the meantime.

If that “interest” is lower than the impact of shipping fast, learning quickly, and unlocking growth, I’ll take that trade every time. However, if that daily interest is higher, then I prefer solving for it right away.

Because if you try to solve every engineering nuance on Day 1, there’s a good chance you’ll never reach the stage where you become big enough to hire the 100th engineer.

Tech debt, like MSME credit, should be used in service of momentum, so you can grow into the company that can repay it quickly and permanently. | 181 | 4 | 2
Nine years into building ProcMart, I’ve sat with hundreds of SMB operators across India.

From manufacturing to trading to distribution, the sectors vary, but their thinking doesn’t. Unlike most startup founders, these operators don’t obsess over CAC, DAUs, or net retention.

Their north star is simpler:
- Do customers come back without being nudged?
- Are we making money on every transaction?

If the answer is yes, they scale. If not, they fix what’s broken. No dashboards needed.

Their playbook is built on first principles:
- Know your margins down to the line item
- Manage credit and cash like survival depends on it
- Build relationships that last decades
- Treat retention as a product outcome, not a marketing campaign

If that “interest” is lower than the impact of shipping fast, learning quickly, and unlocking growth, I’ll take that trade every time. However, if that daily interest is higher, then I prefer solving for it right away.

Because if you try to solve every engineering nuance on Day 1, there’s a good chance you’ll never reach the stage where you become big enough to hire the 100th engineer.

Tech debt, like MSME credit, should be used in service of momentum, so you can grow into the company that can repay it quickly and permanently.
“We will figure margins at scale.”

I hear versions of this often. But I believe, if something is broken, scale amplifies it, it does not fix it. And this applies to broken unit economics as well.

When we decided to step into manufacturing, the first spreadsheet we signed was not a revenue projection, it was the unit economics.

Input costs with realistic variance. Scrap rates at the line, not the brochure. Run‑rate payroll that includes the night shift nobody accounts for. Working capital that assumes a customer will delay payment exactly when you need it most.

Margin‑less growth looks exciting on LinkedIn and press releases, but it looks terrifying on the factory floor. The day you cannot pay for resin or paper because a big invoice is late, is the day strategy meets reality.

Your team deserves better, your customers do too.

If a unit cannot make money, a thousand units will just lose money faster.
I’ve raised over $40M in funding, and I’ve also failed to raise when I thought I had everything right.

The difference is almost always clarity.

Investors don’t live your problem every day. They decide fast. If your pitch doesn’t clearly answer three things in plain English, what problem you solve, who you solve it for, and why it matters now, you lose them.

This is where most founders slip (including me in the early days), they focus on product, not the problem. They talk about features, not outcomes. They stack logos and vision slides, and leave their strongest customer stories buried in the end.

You can be building a brilliant business, but still telling your story wrong.

Fundraising is not that different from enterprise sales. The best pitches I’ve seen start with one operational win that matters to the buyer, repeat it on the last slide, and back it up with retention and frequency of use.

Sure, the story doesn't replace execution, it shows that you have execution. If you can’t tell the story and value in 60 seconds, you’re not ready to raise.
Mediocrity kills startups.

Every culture deck loves “candor, learning, freedom, responsibility.” But none of it works without talent density.

If you compromise on talent density, your company becomes becomes a theatre. People stop solving problems, they just act like they are.

With high talent density, even a mediocre day produces incremental progress. And with low talent density, you're faking momentum, and everyone figures that out eventually.

Always remember that the cost of keeping a misfit is paid by your best people. They carry extra weight, they lose trust in leadership, and eventually they leave.

Build the org around this sequence:
1) hire only people who raise the mean talent density
2) set a brutally clear bar
3) give real autonomy
4) protect the bar with fast feedback and, if you required, decisive exits.

And if you want to check how you are doing in terms of talent density, just ask privately your best performers if they'd rejoin your company today, knowing what they know. If the answer is no, take a hard look at your talent density. Because top talent doesn’t like working around mediocrity.

Oracle just let go of up to 30,000 employees. Despite one of its best quarters in years. 12,000 of them were in India.

We've seen similar waves from Meta, Amazon, Microsoft, Google. The pattern is the same: record profits, and layoffs in the same quarter.

Here's something I've noticed. You know where this isn't happening? In manufacturing, in supply chain, in logistics, in packaging, in warehousing.

These aren't the industries people talk about at parties. They don't make for great LinkedIn bios. But they're also not firing thousands of people while posting record earnings.

There's a massive concentration of tech and AI talent in companies that are already tech-first. And the result of that concentration is exactly this: the more efficient these companies get, the fewer people they need.

Meanwhile, industries like manufacturing are practically greenfield. A factory doing Rs. 50 crore in revenue might still run procurement on WhatsApp. A logistics operator might not have a single dashboard. A packaging company might have no idea what their real unit economics look like.

That's not a problem, that's an opportunity.

If you're a tech professional updating your CV today, consider reaching out to a manufacturer, a warehouse operator, a trucking company. The speed at which you can grow in these spaces is unlike anything in tech. You could even buy one of these businesses and completely transform it.

It won't sound exciting at the next dinner party. But the upside is real, and almost nobody is competing for it.

Piyush Tiwari, SACHIN JAIN, Hitesh Lunawat, Raxit Bisht, Abhishek Kumar Singh, Aman P., Ankur Kandoi, Sanjana Kapoor, Abhishek Rana, krishnananda, Ashutosh Dhar

In every manufacturing plant I've visited, there's one team that gets blamed for everything.

- Production stops. Procurement didn't order on time.
- Costs are high. Procurement should have negotiated better.
- Supplier delivers late. Procurement didn't vet them properly.
- Quality fails. Procurement chose the wrong vendor.

It's always procurement's fault.

I saw this at BHEL. I see it now across 75+ enterprise deployments at ProcMart. The pattern never changes. But here's what nobody says out loud.
- Procurement can't fix a finance team that delays supplier payments by 60 days and then expects vendors to prioritize your orders.
It can't fix engineering that changes specifications after the purchase order is raised.
It can't fix leadership that quietly tolerates maverick buying because "production can't stop."
It can't fix an approval workflow that takes 14 days for a ₹5,000 purchase.

Yet when any of those break, the breakdown shows up in procurement first.

Procurement sits at the intersection of every other function: finance, operations, suppliers, compliance, leadership. When anything goes wrong anywhere, it flows through procurement before it becomes visible.

So procurement gets blamed for problems it didn't create and can't solve alone.

The real issue is never the procurement team. It's that procurement has no single owner above the factory floor who wins when it works and loses when it doesn't.

Until that changes, nothing changes. The same team gets blamed. The same problems repeat and the procurements team would get burned out.

Piyush Tiwari, SACHIN JAIN, Hitesh Lunawat, Raxit Bisht, Abhishek Kumar Singh, Aman P., Ankur Kandoi, Sanjana Kapoor, Abhishek Rana, krishnananda, Ashutosh Dhar

I started my career at BHEL, a government PSU. Now I run a tech startup that helps manufacturers fix their procurement operations.

The decade in between taught me one thing I wish I had known at the start: technology adoption in manufacturing almost never fails because of the technology.

At BHEL, I watched ERP rollouts struggle. The software was solid. The implementation teams were competent. But the factory floor kept doing things the old way. WhatsApp approvals, maverick buying, workarounds that became "how we do things." Nobody had told the procurement manager on the floor why this was his priority. Nobody had agreed on what success even looked like.

Then I built ProcMart and hit the exact same wall from the other side.

75+ enterprise deployments later, the pattern is identical every time. The symptoms look like technology problems. They almost never are.

- "The software isn't being used." Nobody defined what adoption actually means at the floor level.
- "The factory floor resists." Leadership never made it their priority, just their initiative.
- "We need better software." The real problem is behavioral, not functional.
- "ROI isn't showing." Nobody agreed on how to measure it before go-live.

Technology adoption is not a deployment problem. It's a definition and ownership problem. Until someone in leadership personally wins when it succeeds, the factory floor will find a way to survive around it.

The software is rarely the reason it fails. The missing owner almost always is.

Last week I spoke to a candidate who had quit his job without another offer in hand.

He had spent his career in startups. Then he made a deliberate move to a legacy company because they wanted him to lead their AI transformation. He was excited. Real scale, real operations, real impact.

Six months in, he realized AI transformation wasn't actually their priority. It was on the roadmap. It had a budget. But when it competed for leadership attention against quarterly targets, it lost. Every time.

So he quit. Without a backup plan.

I've seen this play out too many times. And honestly, we learned this the hard way at ProcMart.

When we started, we believed that if we built a genuinely useful product, adoption would follow. It didn't. We'd get the contract signed at HQ, then hit a wall at the factory floor. The procurement manager had his own system. His team had their own habits. Nobody had told them this was a priority.

The product wasn't the problem. The buy-in was.

We eventually figured out that two things have to be true for any transformation to actually land. Someone in the top leadership has to own it. And someone in that leadership has to personally win when it succeeds.

If neither is true, the initiative gets polite support and quiet resistance. It never dies officially. It just never really lives either.

That candidate wasn't the problem. He walked into a transformation that leadership had commissioned but never actually prioritized. That's a different thing.

Before taking any transformation role, ask one question: who in the leadership team loses sleep if this fails?

If there's no clear answer, you already know how the story ends.

I grew up around an ecosystem most policy documents call a "legacy industrial cluster."

In Bhilai, the steel plant created the cluster. The cluster created everything else - MSMEs, fabricators, packaging companies, logistics operators. The whole city ran on it. Thousands of families found their footing not because of the plant directly, but because of what grew around it.

Over time, many of these ecosystems stopped compounding. Not because the manufacturing stopped, but because the infrastructure around it didn't evolve - old procurement systems, disconnected suppliers, talent that went to Bangalore instead of staying to build.

The Budget this year announced a scheme to revive 200 such clusters - upgrading infrastructure and technology to make them cost-competitive again. It didn't make headlines the way the semiconductor mission did. But for anyone who grew up in places like Nagpur, Surat, Ludhiana, or Bhilai, it's the line that matters most.

Reviving them isn't a romantic project. It's an economic one. For the same GDP contribution, manufacturing creates far more jobs than services. And most of those jobs live in cities that don't make startup Twitter.

The scheme matters. But what matters more is someone actually showing up on the factory floor to do the work.

That part doesn't have a budget line. It just needs people willing to do it.

The government is putting ₹12.2 lakh crore behind manufacturing this year.

That's a real signal. The kind that changes investment theses, hiring decisions, and where founders choose to build.

But here's something I keep thinking about: every rupee of capex only compounds if the operational layer underneath it works. Roads need suppliers. Factories need procurement. Plants need the invisible infrastructure that nobody photographs.

A few years ago, I visited a bio-fuel plant in Aurangabad. The facility existed, the feedstock existed, the demand existed, but the sourcing was fragmented, cash flow was a mess, and nobody owned the supply chain end to end. The plant was running at a fraction of its potential.

More capital wouldn't have fixed that. Someone had to show up and fix the operations.

India's manufacturing ambition has always been bigger than its infrastructure reality. For the first time, the infrastructure is catching up - ₹12.2 lakh crore in capex, semiconductor missions. legacy cluster revival, biofuel policy, container manufacturing schemes.

The hardware is being built.

The question now is whether the software, the procurement, the supplier networks, the operational discipline, keeps pace.

The factories are coming. The supply chains need to be ready for them.

That's where the real work is.

Last year I met a founder who had raised a large seed round about a year ago.

He should have been energized. Instead, he told me he wanted to quit and build something bootstrapped. Not because he was tired of the work. He was tired of the race: the pitch cycles, the investor updates, the pressure to show metrics that justified the next round before he'd figured out if the current one was working.

That conversation stayed with me.

I've come to believe that capital is a multiplier. But a multiplier only works if there's something real underneath it. Multiply zero and you get zero. Multiply a broken unit economics model and you get a bigger broken model, faster.

When we started ProcMart, we made a deliberate choice not to raise external capital until we knew what we were trying to multiply. For five years, we fixed the fundamentals. Built the supplier network. Figured out where we actually made money. It was slow and unglamorous, and I watched peers raise large rounds and move faster on paper.

But raising capital before you have something worth multiplying is like building large walls around an empty plot. The fort looks impressive. There's nothing inside worth protecting.

When we finally raised, we knew exactly which levers to pull. The capital did what capital is supposed to do: it accelerated something that was already working.

The founder I met last year wasn't weak. He had just spent two years building walls. Nobody had asked him what was inside.

You compete for market share when you can't figure out how to grow the market.

If your success requires taking customers from others, you've already admitted you can't create new value. Early at ProcMart, I realized we weren’t competing with other platforms at all. We were competing with the barriers that kept 50,000 manufacturers out of organized procurement.

Those manufacturers weren't choosing between us and competitors. They were choosing jugaad, WhatsApp approvals, Excel sheets, maverick buying, because organized procurement felt impossible.

Markets look fixed because incumbents build for people who already have access, budgets, and literacy. That creates the illusion of zero-sum competition. Every "market size" is actually a measure of "how many people can access the solution with current tools." But when you eliminate the barriers that artificially constrain markets, the market explodes.

Shopify didn't compete for the 40,000 existing online stores. They made it possible for millions who couldn't afford stores to create them. The market wasn't 40,000 stores. It was everyone who wanted to sell something but couldn't.

The history of technology is the history of abundance replacing scarcity. Online retail didn't take market share from malls. It created entirely new purchasing behavior. Every major company grew the pie, they didn't fight over it.

So the real question isn’t, “How do we beat competitors?”
It’s this, "What prevents non-customers from becoming customers, and how do we eliminate it?"

Answer that, and competition becomes noise. Ignore it, and you'll spend your career fighting over scraps while someone else feeds the world.

The biggest mistake I see companies make is hiring "strategic thinkers" who are too senior to get their hands dirty.

When I hire leaders at ProcMart, I look for one thing above everything else. And it's not pedigree, years of experience, or even domain expertise. I look for people who can't stay away from the details

Leaders who want to be in deployment calls. Who visit factories without being asked. Who review implementation docs because they care about getting it right.

In B2B manufacturing, strategic thinking siloed from operational reality is useless. You can't build a good strategy if you don't understand what's actually happening on factory floors.

I'd rather hire someone junior who obsesses over details than someone senior who delegates everything and reviews dashboards. Because excellence lives in details. And you can't maintain excellence if you're not in the details yourself.

If you're building a B2B company, hire people who care enough to stay operationally involved. Everything else can be taught.

Piyush Tiwari, SACHIN JAIN, Hitesh Lunawat, Raxit Bisht, Abhishek Kumar Singh, Aman P., Ankur Kandoi, Sanjana Kapoor, Abhishek Rana, krishnananda, Ashutosh Dhar

If your product needs extensive training, you’ve failed at design.

Complexity in the problem does not require complexity in the interface. I’m not against training, but users shouldn’t need it just to complete basic tasks.

When a factory manager tells me, “I’m not technical enough for this,” that’s my failure as a builder. Not his limitation as a user. He should be able to create his first purchase order in five minute, without training, without documentation, and without calling support.

One of the biggest mistakes organizations make when implementing new technology is assuming employees will intuitively understand how the system works.

We work with 75+ large enterprises and our platform handles multi-location procurement, complex approval workflows, thousands of suppliers, and compliance tracking. It's complex, but it lives on the backend. The procurement manager on the factory floor just sees a clean interface that matches how he already thinks about buying.

The complexity is our job to handle. Not his job to comprehend.

Every time someone says, “users need to learn how enterprise systems work,” I translate, “We couldn’t figure out how to make it intuitive.”

The goal isn’t to make users smarter about your software. The goal is to make your software smarter about your users.

Piyush Tiwari, SACHIN JAIN, Hitesh Lunawat, Raxit Bisht, Abhishek Kumar Singh, Aman P., Ankur Kandoi, Sanjana Kapoor, Abhishek Rana, krishnananda, Ashutosh Dhar

I used to think we were building a procurement marketplace. I was wrong.

Last year, we met with one of India’s largest conglomerates. They could easily get better rates than us on packaging and MRO. In some categories, they already do.

But they still chose ProcMart.

Not for cheaper procurement, but for our ability to understand their workflows deeply. To solutionize faster. To define approval processes their teams could deploy without reinventing the wheel.

Over 10 years and 75+ enterprise deployments, we’ve accumulated intelligence that can’t be replicated overnight. We understand where procurement breaks. We know which approval structures actually work. We’ve seen what fails.

That accumulated knowledge is our moat. Not our supplier network. Not our tech stack. Our intelligence from implementations.

Every deployment teaches us something new. Every failed workflow becomes a recognizable pattern. Every successful rollout adds to our playbook.

India’s manufacturing is expected to grow from 14% to 21% of GDP by 2032. As this happens, manufacturers won’t want to be the first to figure things out. They’ll want partners who’ve already lived their problems.

Your pricing can be matched. Your features can be copied. But the intelligence you build by solving real problems for real customers, that compounds.

Piyush Tiwari, SACHIN JAIN, Hitesh Lunawat, Raxit Bisht, Abhishek Kumar Singh, Aman P., Ankur Kandoi, Sanjana Kapoor, Abhishek Rana, krishnananda, Ashutosh Dhar

Studying competition is the fastest way to irrelevance.

It doesn't make you faster, it makes you a derivative. While you're analyzing their race line, they're analyzing yours, and you're both losing time to whoever's staring at the track itself.

Sure, you need to have market intelligence, but it comes in two forms:
1. Customer intelligence (proactive & keeps compounding)
2. Competitor intelligence (reactive & makes you a derivative)

We made a choice early on in ProcMart, every dollar that could go to competitive analysis would go to understanding customers instead.

For this reason, I don't believe in battle cards, you don't need feature comparisons, you need to have customer problem maps. You need to document every customer crisis you have solved, everytime you've failed (and won). That becomes your deployment playbook and your roadmap.

Last month, a customer told us they chose ProcMart because we defined their problem better than anyone else, and could predict what would break before it did.

That's why I keep saying, customer obsession isn't a marketing slogan. It's an operational choice about where you invest your time and attention.

I'd rather have deep knowledge of 50 customer problems than surface knowledge of 500 competitor features. Because the former builds moat, the latter just keeps you busy.

Piyush Tiwari, SACHIN JAIN, Hitesh Lunawat, Raxit Bisht, Abhishek Kumar Singh, Aman P., Ankur Kandoi, Sanjana Kapoor, Abhishek Rana, krishnananda, Ashutosh Dhar, Anshu (Sudhanshu) Srivastav

Someone asked me recently, “How do you find your passion?”

I don’t think you find it. I think it finds you. When I started ProcMart, I wasn’t chasing a trend. I wasn’t looking at market opportunity slides. I was solving a problem I had lived.

During my BHEL days, I saw procurement chaos every single day. MRO grinding plants to a halt, maverick buying, and no accountability. I hated it at the time.

But that frustration, that “this shouldn’t be this hard” feeling, became my conviction. I couldn’t have manufactured that. No amount of market research would have given me that insight.

Here’s what I believe now. You can’t force product market fit in yourself either. If you’re building something because it looks exciting, or because investors are interested, or because everyone else is doing it, you’ll give up the moment it gets hard.

But if you’re building something because you can’t not build it, because you’ve seen the problem up close and it bothers you every day, you won’t stop.

That’s not passion. That’s obsession born from lived experience. And you don’t need motivation when you’ve actually lived the problem. You need solutions.

So if you’re looking for your passion, stop looking. Go live in the messiest, most chaotic version of the industry you’re curious about. Your passion will find you in that chaos.

A prospect asked us last month, "Why shouldn't we just go directly to your suppliers?"

My instinct was to defend. To list our value adds. To talk about our platform, our tech, our network. I didn't.

Instead, I said, "You absolutely can. Let me walk you through what will happen." I took him through it. Step by step.

First, you'll need to identify which suppliers are reliable. You'll call 50, maybe 5 will respond. Then you'll negotiate terms. They'll agree to 15 days, deliver in 30. You'll have no recourse because there's no contract accountability. Meanwhile, your line stops. Fingers get pointed. You firefight.

By the end, he said, "I see why this doesn't work."

I didn't sell him ProcMart. He sold himself.

Here's what I learned from someone much wiser than me. When you spoon-feed, you insult your audience. When you let them discover, they feel smart. And when they feel smart, they trust you.

This applies everywhere. In sales, in team building and in manufacturing. If you're explaining every single detail, you're not respecting their intelligence. You're treating them like they can't figure it out.

The best founders, the best salespeople, the best manufacturers don't fill in all the blanks. They create space for realization. Because people don't buy what you tell them. They buy what they discover for themselves.

ProcMart turns 10 this week.

I’m writing this not to talk about our growth, profits, or valuation, but to reflect on how these ten years have shaped us.

Looking back, I don’t think individual brilliance or some maverick move got us here. What actually did was our stubborn refusal to give up. Quarter after quarter, year after year, we just kept showing up. There were no dramatic turning points, just small, steady improvements. But those small improvements opened new geographies, new categories, funding, revenue… everything.

One thing I’ve come to believe is the importance of being flexible with the approach, but unshakeable about the problem you're solving. A customer told me a few months ago, “You’ve been saying the same thing for the last eight years.” And I said, “That's the point.” When things don’t work, it’s easy to change the goal to something simpler or safer. But most of the time, it’s not the goal that’s wrong, it’s the way we’re trying to get there.

This mindset now defines us. Before stepping away from a category, geography, or even a person, we ask, “Are we just approaching this the wrong way? What else can we try?”

As we hit the 10 year mark, I am grateful to my customers who trusted us early, to my team who stood by me through everything, and to our investors who believed this problem was worth solving.

There’s no big finish here, just a quiet acknowledgment of how far we’ve come, and confidence that the next decade will demand the same grit, patience, and willingness to try again.

Piyush Tiwari, SACHIN JAIN, Hitesh Lunawat, Raxit Bisht, Abhishek Kumar Singh, Aman P., Ankur Kandoi, Sanjana Kapoor, Abhishek Rana, krishnananda, Ashutosh Dhar

Ten years into building ProcMart, I've noticed something about profitability that nobody talks about.

It's not just a financial milestone. It's a clarity filter.

When I talk to founders, I hear justifications for losses: "We're building infrastructure." "Network effects take time." Both can be true. But they're also convenient.

Profitability forces a different question: Is what we're doing actually creating value that customers will pay for?

That question kills features that sound clever but serve two customers. Expansion ideas that look like growth but have no path to unit economics. Investments that boost activity but not contribution.

What survives gets sharper. Supplier onboarding that reduces friction. Automation that makes service better and costs lower. Focus on categories where you've built real depth.

I've also seen this change who joins companies. The best builders don't ask about funding rounds anymore. They ask about margins, about what customers value, about whether the business can sustain itself.

Because profit isn't anti-growth. It's anti-confusion.

Capital accelerates momentum. But profitability is what proves you have momentum worth accelerating.

A few years ago, I met an MSME operator who'd been running his business for 18 years.

Single product line. Steady revenue around ₹40 crore. When I asked why he never diversified, he said, "Each new thing is a 30% tax on the original thing."

I didn't fully understand it then. But I do now.

At ProcMart, we get asked often if we solve for direct procurement too. It's tempting, quick wins, bigger deals, top-line boost.

But I've noticed that every time we've chased opportunities outside indirect procurement, our core business slows down. Not dramatically, but enough to matter. Because focus isn't just about what you do. It's about protecting what you're building from dilution.

That operator was right. When you add something new, it doesn't just require resources, it taxes everything you've already built. Your team's attention. Your supplier relationships. Your clarity about who you serve.

Now when we expand into new categories, I try to do some of the heavy lifting myself before it becomes someone's full-time role. They get the leverage of everything we've built, our suppliers, our systems, our distribution, but they don't drain mindspace from our cash-generating businesses.

Their blast radius stays small. What's working keeps working.

Saying no is hard, especially when the opportunity looks good. But staying focused lets your work compound in one direction instead of fragmenting across three.

Might sound old school. But it works.

In high-trust B2B, your story bank is your only moat.

Features are commoditized in 18 months. Stories compound for decades.

In 2019, we lost a deal to a competitor. It hurt. Later, that same procurement head called us back. Their vendor had perfect uptime but zero empathy during a crisis.

He said: "You told me about the bearing that failed at your customer's plant and how your team drove 4 hours to deliver a replacement at midnight. I need a partner who's lived my chaos, not just studied it."

That's the power of stories built from lived experiences.

Every failure you survived. Every emergency you solved. Every customer who called you first when things broke. These become un-replicable because they required time and pain.

Competitors can copy your features in months. They can match your pricing. They can hire your engineers. But they can't copy 9 years of 2 AM phone calls answered. They can't simulate the credibility of showing up for your customers.

This is the arbitrage nobody talks about: while everyone optimizes their pitch deck, the real compounding happens in the stories only time can write.

Your positioning isn't what you claim. It's what you've survived.

Our biggest competitor isn't another SaaS platform.

It's the Excel sheet the procurement manager has maintained for 7 years.

He knows every formula. Every workaround. Every vendor's personal number is in there. It's ugly, broken, and irreplaceable, because it's his.

When we pitch ProcMart, we're not competing on features. We're competing against his identity.

That Excel sheet represents his expertise. Moving to our platform means admitting: "My system doesn't work." For a 20-year veteran, that's not a product decision, it's an identity crisis.

I learned this when a plant head rejected us despite his CEO approving budget. He said: "Your software might be better. But my team knows my way."

So we changed the pitch. Instead of replacing his Excel, we positioned as building on top of what he'd already created. Import his sheet. Keep his logic. Add our automation as a layer that makes his system more powerful.

Adoption jumped 3x.

Here's the insight: People don't resist change, they resist loss. Loss of control. Loss of credit. Loss of the identity they've built.

Every market has an invisible incumbent. It's not always a competitor. Sometimes it's just the customer's existing solution to their own problem.

Beat the product, but honour the person who built it.

Piyush Tiwari, SACHIN JAIN, Hitesh Lunawat, Raxit Bisht, Abhishek Kumar Singh, Aman P., Ankur Kandoi, Sanjana Kapoor, Abhishek Rana, krishnananda, Ashutosh Dhar

When I joined BHEL, they hadn't hired freshers for a few years before our batch.

That created what I'd call an experience arbitrage. Suddenly, people in their early twenties were sitting in front of senior leaders, customers, and vendors, making decisions, facing heat, solving problems we had no textbooks for.

I hated it at the time - long hours, real pressure. Not what you expect from government jobs.

But looking back, that accelerated my learning by at least a decade.

It taught me something I carry to this day: coachability matters more than experience.

You don't just bet on someone who's at the peak of their career. You bet when they're on the slope, when they're learning fast, adapting quickly, and hungry to grow.

Even now at ProcMart, some of our early team members surprise me with how quickly they pick up new problems and solve them. It's become muscle memory for them. They have learned to find answers fast.

The problems we're solving in manufacturing procurement don't have textbooks yet. We're writing the playbook as we go.

Smart and hardworking are not differentiator, you can always find smart and hard-working people, and with AI, the experience is getting commoditised.

But being coachable is what lets someone grow with the company instead of plateauing.

“We will figure margins at scale.”

I hear versions of this often. But I believe, if something is broken, scale amplifies it, it does not fix it. And this applies to broken unit economics as well.

When we decided to step into manufacturing, the first spreadsheet we signed was not a revenue projection, it was the unit economics.

Input costs with realistic variance. Scrap rates at the line, not the brochure. Run‑rate payroll that includes the night shift nobody accounts for. Working capital that assumes a customer will delay payment exactly when you need it most.

Margin‑less growth looks exciting on LinkedIn and press releases, but it looks terrifying on the factory floor. The day you cannot pay for resin or paper because a big invoice is late, is the day strategy meets reality.

Your team deserves better, your customers do too.

If a unit cannot make money, a thousand units will just lose money faster.

I keep coming across manufacturing plants handling procurements with "jugaad."

It’s WhatsApp approvals at midnight, it’s a vendor’s man-on-the-ground rushing to source material, it’s someone going rogue with maverick buying because “production can’t stop.”

The reality is, no amount of software training will fix this.

You can deploy the best tools. You can integrate every supplier, automate POs, and standardize catalogs. But none of that matters if there isn’t one accountable layer owning the indirect procurement process. Without ownership, the software becomes just another reporting tool, not a control mechanism.

The deeper truth is: indirect procurement isn't a software problem; it's a behavioural, operational, cash-flow, trust and accountability problem.

What it really needs is a connective tissue between IT, Finance, Procurement, Suppliers, and even Financiers. That connective tissue brings accountability to indirect buying. It ensures that the deployment doesn't remain a training exercise, it also results in cost savings, improved efficiency.

That connective tissue is ProcMart, helping manufacturers look beyond the "jugaad."

I’ve raised over $40M in funding, and I’ve also failed to raise when I thought I had everything right.

The difference is almost always clarity.

Investors don’t live your problem every day. They decide fast. If your pitch doesn’t clearly answer three things in plain English, what problem you solve, who you solve it for, and why it matters now, you lose them.

This is where most founders slip (including me in the early days), they focus on product, not the problem. They talk about features, not outcomes. They stack logos and vision slides, and leave their strongest customer stories buried in the end.

You can be building a brilliant business, but still telling your story wrong.

Fundraising is not that different from enterprise sales. The best pitches I’ve seen start with one operational win that matters to the buyer, repeat it on the last slide, and back it up with retention and frequency of use.

Sure, the story doesn't replace execution, it shows that you have execution. If you can’t tell the story and value in 60 seconds, you’re not ready to raise.

I grew up in a small city, Bhilai.

It’s known as the Steel City of Central India because of the Bhilai Steel Plant, where my father worked. I grew up in the staff quarters, surrounded by a community shaped by industry.

Thanks to the steel plant, Bhilai became home to thousands of MSMEs, small manufacturers, logistics companies, packaging, fabricators, and more.

What always amazed me was how instinctively these business owners manage credit. Ten-day, fifteen-day, thirty-day credit lines, all negotiated, honored, and optimized, with the cost of credit blended into every transaction, every quote, every decision.

No one teaches them, it’s in their bloodstream. That’s how they scale.

I’ve always believed tech founders should treat tech debt the same way, as a credit line.

If an engineering problem can be solved by our 100th engineer, I prefer leaving it for them, as long as I can afford the 'daily interest' of semi-automated or manual workflows in the meantime.

If that “interest” is lower than the impact of shipping fast, learning quickly, and unlocking growth, I’ll take that trade every time. However, if that daily interest is higher, then I prefer solving for it right away.

Because if you try to solve every engineering nuance on Day 1, there’s a good chance you’ll never reach the stage where you become big enough to hire the 100th engineer.

Tech debt, like MSME credit, should be used in service of momentum, so you can grow into the company that can repay it quickly and permanently.

Nine years into building ProcMart, I’ve sat with hundreds of SMB operators across India.

From manufacturing to trading to distribution, the sectors vary, but their thinking doesn’t. Unlike most startup founders, these operators don’t obsess over CAC, DAUs, or net retention.

Their north star is simpler:
- Do customers come back without being nudged?
- Are we making money on every transaction?

If the answer is yes, they scale. If not, they fix what’s broken. No dashboards needed.

Their playbook is built on first principles:
- Know your margins down to the line item
- Manage credit and cash like survival depends on it
- Build relationships that last decades
- Treat retention as a product outcome, not a marketing campaign

There's no shortcut or growth hack, just consistent value creation.

And because many of these are generational businesses, a short-term win that breaks trust can cost them not just a customer today, but an entire legacy.

That kind of value obsession compounds over time. Customers come back. They bring others. And scale happens, not through hacks or clever campaigns, but through trust.

It might sound boring or old school, but it works.

Dashboards help. But durable companies are built on one truth: If people return on their own and pay you fair value for your product or service, you’re onto something. If they don’t, nothing else matters.

In high context markets, buyers do not just question your product.

They ask one question silently: “Will these people show up when it hurts?”

That's the reason founders, business leaders and even product leaders have to be obsessed with stories. Because, stories travel faster and further than feature lists, number of suppliers or categories.

That's why, when I pitch ProcMart, I do not start with tech. I start with the factory, the people, and the decisions we made when no one was watching. That is what convinces a procurement head that we will still pick up the phone on a holiday.

Find your storyteller early.

In our case, bringing in senior leaders who can tell the customer’s story back to them changed our trajectory. Because customers are not buying features, they are finding a partner who will share risk, shield their operations from noise, and admit a mistake before it becomes a crisis.

In a noisy world, your story is your differentiator.

Sales isn’t pushing harder or pitching smarter. It’s making the other person feel, “You get my mess, and you know the shortest path out.”

When I started ProcMart, I had zero experience in building or selling software. But I did know how MRO could grind an entire plant to a halt.

During my stint at BHEL, I oversaw ERP rollouts across units. Direct procurement was clean - raw material, packaging, PO in, PO out. But MRO was chaos. There were fragmented catalogs, 5 different ways for the same vendors, maverick buying, and purchase requests stuck behind approvals, while the line waited.

So when I sat down with my first prospect, I didn’t “pitch.” I just told them what their day looked like: their calls to vendors who never answered, their workaround on spreadsheets that nobody trusted. And they nodded, not because I’m a great storyteller, but because I was telling THEIR story.

One of our earliest demos broke, due to a bug in the product. But the procurement head still gave us the order. Not because the product was perfect, but because he wanted us to win as he believed we were solving his version of the problem, not a generic one.

Today, we have 75+ large enterprises on ProcMart. Our biggest asset isn’t a slide or a feature list. It’s the library of problems we’ve seen, fixed, and our ability to speak in plain language, those stories can't be copied. We've lived them over the last 9 years.

This is what “leading with story” really means: it’s not about theatrics. It’s about the real details. The kind only someone who’s lived it can share.

We overestimate what we do in a month, and underestimate what we can do in 10 years.

And that leaves us in a constant state of comparing ourselves with others, without understanding how long they've been compounding quietly behind the scenes.

On day 1 of building anything, there's no leverage, so for the same effort you get much lower outputs than you would get after building leverage for 1, 5 or 10 years.

I recall when I started, we were working so hard - negotiating with suppliers, selling to MNCs, raising capital, building product, shipping stuff in my own car... but all that barely moved the needle. I could always see my peers marching much ahead in their lives. And that often makes you ask existential questions as well, that make you feel you're behind schedule.

Because building leverage is invisible, it compounds painfully slowly at first. Then, all at once.

Today, no matter what product line we launch or which category we expand to, we get to a few crores of revenue almost instantly. The same revenue might have taken us 2 years when we started, and it's because now we have the leverage - network, distribution, skills, vendors, team, and our name.

So, if you're starting up - your company or your career, just think of building leverage. Growth will follow.

Founder-led hiring is highly underrated.

When you’re running a startup, it’s tempting to delegate hiring early. You’ve got a thousand things pulling for your attention, and hiring feels like something a HR team should handle.

But hiring right is a make or break for startups, because your team is the company. The quality of every hire determines whether you grow or stall. Which means as a founder, hiring is the one thing you cannot outsource too quickly.

Our HR team is solid but top talent is bombarded by recruiters every day, a founder reaching out to them catches their attention. Also, I believe no one else can sell the mission like a founder. No one else can paint the picture of where we’re heading with the same conviction.

It’s not just about “filling a role.” It’s about:
- Showing the kind of urgency and obsession we expect from our people
- Signaling to candidates that joining us is not just a job, it’s a bet on a vision
- Making sure talent density compounds as we scale

Founder-led hiring builds credibility. It attracts people who want to work with you, not just “a company.” And that’s what you need if you want to build something enduring.

We just onboarded a bunch of really solid operators to boost our leadership, and I am certain that one of the reasons they joined us is because it was me pitching our vision to them.

Sales isn’t pushing harder or pitching smarter. It’s making the other person feel, “You get my mess, and you know the shortest path out.”

When I started ProcMart, I had zero experience in building or selling software. But I did know how MRO could grind an entire plant to a halt.

During my stint at BHEL, I oversaw ERP rollouts across units. Direct procurement was clean - raw material, packaging, PO in, PO out. But MRO was chaos. There were fragmented catalogs, 5 different ways for the same vendors, maverick buying, and purchase requests stuck behind approvals, while the line waited.

So when I sat down with my first prospect, I didn’t “pitch.” I just told them what their day looked like: their calls to vendors who never answered, their workaround on spreadsheets that nobody trusted. And they nodded, not because I’m a great storyteller, but because I was telling THEIR story.

One of our earliest demos broke, due to a bug in the product. But the procurement head still gave us the order. Not because the product was perfect, but because he wanted us to win as he believed we were solving his version of the problem, not a generic one.
