Today, we have 75+ large enterprises on ProcMart. Our biggest asset isn’t a slide or a feature list. It’s the library of problems we’ve seen, fixed, and our ability to speak in plain language, those stories can't be copied. We've lived them over the last 9 years.

This is what “leading with story” really means: it’s not about theatrics. It’s about the real details. The kind only someone who’s lived it can share.

AI is rewriting how we value companies, and even price our products.

Building software or distribution is not a permanent advantage anymore. With time, others can match them. The real moat for companies is their shared memory and context.

This accumulated knowledge compounds into intelligence. And it’s that intelligence, the ability to understand, adapt, and improve, that customers truly value. It’s also what ultimately drives long-term valuations.

When we started ProcMart, our edge was our supplier network. But over time, what began to really capture mindshare was the intelligence we built through countless implementations. We understood workflows, we could decode internal processes, we knew where bottlenecks were hiding, and, more importantly, how to fix them. We weren’t just moving transactions anymore. We were uplifting operations for both customers and suppliers.

A few months ago, I met with one of the largest manufacturers in India. They were clear: they could get better commercials on their own. But they still signed up with us, not for pricing, but for transformation. They wanted to become a technology-first procurement organization. And we had the intelligence to get them there.

That proved our thesis - if you only move transactions, you’ll be relevant for a while. But if you build knowledge others rely on, you’ll stay relevant for the long run.

That’s why our goal now is to keep compounding intelligence. And that’s also why we’re entering manufacturing ourselves. Because there’s only so much you can optimize from the outside. At some point, to keep raising the ceiling, you have to own the floor.

Cost arbitrage won’t help India win the global manufacturing race, and here’s why we shouldn’t even try.

China earned its title as the factory of the world by starting with a cost advantage. In the ’90s, a “Made in China” label often meant flimsy plastic; today, it sits on surgical robots and EV batteries.

What changed wasn’t labor costs, it was infrastructure compounding over decades: world-class ports and highways, dense supplier networks, rigorous QC labs, vocational schools.

Scale funded sophistication, and sophistication reshaped China’s brand. Every serious manufacturing nation follows that playbook: begin with cost, evolve toward quality, reliability, and ecosystem control.

Now, as India pushes its manufacturing, there’s a temptation to copy the first half of the playbook: compete on cost. But I believe, that’s a bad strategy and we need to entirely skip the 'low quality era' because cheaper labor won’t shield us from inefficiency, and subsidies won’t compensate for unreliability.

That means:
- Logistics that work before the order is urgent.
- Procurement that’s predictable, not personality-driven.
- Founders who think like infrastructure architects, not just factory operators.

At ProcMart, we’re building the procurement layer, but the India manufacturing stack needs builders across logistics, capital, and compliance.

India can’t win by being China-2005. We win by becoming India-2030, efficient, trusted, and built on reliability, not just cost.

Early in ProcMart, I believed I was “developing talent” because I held regular mentoring sessions

My early team showed up every week with advice from me but not much changed. So, soon out of sheer work volume, I started handing over ownership to my team - Ankur, Raxit, Abhishek, Hitesh, Abhishek and others.

Instead of parachuting in myself in every problem, I told my customers: “Raxit owns this. Treat his call as mine.” Honestly, even I was nervous, “Too big, too soon.”

Mentor‑me would have given them confidence quotes. Sponsor‑me gave them the stage and air cover as they learned in public.

Mentor vs Sponsor
- Mentor: private advice.
- Sponsor: public commitment. Puts personal reputation on the line. Shields early stumbles so growth compounds.

Now, I’m introducing a new KPI at ProcMart: “Who did you sponsor outside your own mirror?”

Because affinity bias is real, left unchecked we only elevate younger versions of ourselves. And manufacturing in India will not scale on clones.

If you lead people, pick one high‑intent colleague who isn’t the obvious choice. Attach your credibility and let them borrow your brand until they build their own.

OpenAI just launched its consulting business, and they're charging $10M+ to enterprises for their 'deployment-as-a-service' model!

And now, all of a sudden, getting into services and consulting has become a lot cooler, even for product companies. It's a welcome change.

The real value for enterprises is provided not by the tech stack or API access; it's in driving transformation, and that requires a consulting-first mindset. You can have the best UI and integrations, but if your customer likes to place orders on WhatsApp and you don't know that, you're looking at a failed deployment and, inevitably, churn.

And if global enterprises and tech companies require handholding to implement technology, imagine implementing something in a factory and expecting them to figure it out on their own. Never happens.

That's why we had split our post-sales team into two parts:
- Managing relationships with procurement leaders and HQs
- Managing relationships on the factory floors and driving the change bottom-up

Of course, driving anything bottom-up isn't exciting, but that's what moves the needle once you get the go-ahead from HQs. The top-down buy-in gets you the contract; the bottom-up traction delivers the impact (and renewals).

If you're not making money at unit level, you're running a hobby, not business.

Factories have been buying for packaging, MRO and bio-fuel. These needs are essential & baseline needs. Manufacturers will continue to buy, with or without us. This means, demand is not the differentiator.

When a startup like ours enters the picture, our value lies in enhancing efficiency, reducing costs, adding a layer of reliability and experience. That’s what we offer, but let’s be clear: we are an alternative, not the origin of demand. That’s a fact!

Therefore, simply being in the flow of a transaction (without capturing margin or adding monetizable value) doesn't make you a business, it makes you an observer with a high burn rate.

Yes, revenue might go up. Yes, client logos might stack up. Yes, there's a "strategic" story you can sell to investors about future value.

But let’s not kid ourselves: Zero margin on 1 crore, 100 crore, or 1000 crore is same. Zero.

Acquiring customers and routing enterprise spend through our platform is not a business model unless there's a path to profitability, at the unit level.

Strategic bets are valid, but only if they come with a clear, time-bound path to profitability. Otherwise, we’re just subsidising existing behaviour, not changing it. And that’s not business. That’s a very expensive hobby.

Why are you getting into manufacturing? That's a common question I keep coming across. Here's why:

Manufacturing gives us a seat at the table, alongside MNCs, Indian conglomerates, and the very manufacturers we serve.

You can’t build a legacy just by staying a trader. To truly add value, shape India’s manufacturing future, and understand what manufacturers really need, you have to be in the game, not just on the sidelines.

Is it hard? Absolutely. But that’s exactly what makes it meaningful, and irreplaceable.

For far too long, India’s top talent has stayed away from manufacturing. That’s one reason why our manufacturing output in GDP is just half of China’s. We want to change that.

And despite what many think, manufacturing isn’t as risky as it’s made out to be. It’s not an overnight win, but it’s a clear path to a home run. A longer payback, yes, but a sure one.

I am building ProcMart to outlive me.

Knowing who you're really competing against is fundamental to building any business. But too often, we reduce our worldview to just the players in our direct space - other startups, platforms, technology plays.

Take Netflix's example: "Our biggest competitor is sleep." They're not fighting just for screen-time of the user, but the time itself.

In our case, we are not competing with other startups, we are competing with the traditional suppliers & operators - people who have built their businesses on trust & long-standing relationships. They show up, they know everyone on the factory floor, they understand that being reliable is not a feature, it's a promise. They don't say, 'It'll happen in X working days." They just make it happen.

That's the real bar!

So if we can’t ensure availability, and instead hide behind promised timelines and logistics jargons, customers will default to the status quo. They’ll go back to what’s familiar, because what’s familiar also works.

Technology gives us scale, visibility, and process, but relationship-building, showing up & earning trust is non-negotiable.

Over time, I’ve realised that ProcMart's unfair advantage is this: we think like a tech startup, but our customer obsession matches the best of the traditional suppliers we aim to unify on our platform.

You can not pay your way into product-market-fit, especially in B2B.

B2B adoption takes time, especially in industries like manufacturing, bio-fuel, packaging & MRO. I've seen companies operating at zero margins, and even at negative margins to win deals and acquire customers.

Yet I keep seeing startups trying to force their way into the market - offering deals at zero or even negative margins just to land customers. The thinking is, “Let’s win logos now, figure out margins later.”

We forget that:
- B2B buyers are not impulse shoppers - they don't sign up for discounts, they pay for reliability, quality and long term service.
- Burning VC money to win deals is not sustainable - unlike B2C, where customer's LTV becomes predictable at scale, B2B deal cycles are long and the ACVs are high.

And most importantly, once you start rewarding teams for chasing GMV over profits, you create a dangerous mindset. It leads to bad pricing decisions, poor customer fit, and eventually a fragile business.

Product-market fit in B2B isn’t about who can give the biggest discount. It’s about solving real problems, delivering consistent value, and building sustainable partnerships.

Margin-less growth is a strategy of denial, not scale!

Let’s grow ProcMart together, Krishna!

Krishna and I go way back, we were roommates during our stint at BHEL, both set on a stable (if slightly too predictable) government job path. Honestly, for the first two years, I genuinely thought I’d retire from there :)

But life had other plans, and so did we.

I’m excited to welcome Krishna onboard as Chief Growth Officer at ProcMart. And I’ll admit, I’m a little envious of his role. Hunting for new business opportunities, driving strategic acquisitions, and leading multiple zero-to-one journeys - this is the kind of work every founder secretly (or not so secretly) loves doing!

For this role, I needed a true hustler, someone who’s wired to move fast, push boundaries, and make things happen. And who better than a guy who once quit a secure government job to chase the unpredictable, thrilling world of startups?

Welcome aboard, krishnananda. Let’s build an incredible company together.

We recently elevated SACHIN to co-founder.

I always tell my investors, my employees & even my customers that often the most obvious end user won't get our value as they don't know what they don't know. And you have to show them the value - handhold them, tell them success stories and become their advisor.

That's exactly what Sachin also did - when he joined us, he showed us what we didn't know, he brought structure to our chaos, and he showed us a path to predictable growth. I honestly didn't even know that a CFO is supposed to do all that!

He’s been a sounding board, a strategist, a builder, and someone who has leaned into the business with as much intensity and ownership as any founder would. So, elevating him as co-founder of ProcMart was the most obvious and natural thing to do!

I’ve come to believe that the best CFOs don’t stay “just CFOs.” They grow into operators, advisors, partners, and eventually, into the co-pilots founders need to build something lasting.

All the best, Sachin! I know the title won’t change how you show up, and I have no doubt you’ll continue to raise the bar you’ve already set so high.

India overtakes Japan as the 4th largest economy, but our best is yet to come!

What’s really exciting about India becoming the world’s 4th largest economy is not just the ranking, it’s the massive headroom for growth that's still there for us.

Take manufacturing, for example. While China’s manufacturing sector contributes around 27% to its GDP, India’s manufacturing share is only about 14%. This gap is our opportunity: India has the potential to double its manufacturing output.

Even a modest rise to 25% manufacturing share could inject hundreds of billions of dollars into the economy, potentially pushing India into the Top 3 global economies, overtaking Germany next.

On top of this, India has one of the youngest workforces in the world. With the right policies, infrastructure, and skilling programs, this demographic advantage can become the engine of a manufacturing-led economic boom.

We’re standing at the point where our climb has just begun!

India’s growth story can’t just rely on its services sector.

The services sector contributes to over 30% of India’s GDP and has been major factor in India's urban development - building cities like Gurgaon, Noida, Coimbatore, Mohali, Jaipur, Pune, and many others.

But there’s another story, quieter, yet just as important.

India's manufacturing sector.

Contributing around over 14% of GDP, it has helped decongest top cities by powering growth in places like Jamshedpur, Bokaro, Surat, Nagpur, Jamnagar, Ludhiana, and my hometown, Bhilai.

For every rupee of GDP, manufacturing creates more jobs than services. And these jobs are not limited to metro cities, they are spread across Tier-2 and Tier-3 towns.

A single factory in a Tier-2 town can support hundreds of families, local schools, and small businesses. It gives dignity, opportunity, and stability to regions often left behind.

So when people ask me:
“What do you want to achieve in the next 10–15 years?”
My answer is clear: Raise manufacturing’s share of GDP to 30%.

But no one can do this alone. It will take a collective push - from founders, investors, policymakers, and talent.

This isn’t about ProcMart or any one company. This is about growing the entire pie so there’s space for thousands of manufacturers and their enablers.

If India truly wants to be self-reliant, manufacturing must step up.

From joining us straight out of college to becoming a pillar of strength and growth at ProcMart, Raxit has come a long way.

I’m deeply grateful for everything he brings to the table. It’s truly inspiring to see how he has consistently upped his game, year after year: making us a force to reckon with!

Storytime:

In the early days of ProcMart, many manufacturers we worked with saw us as just another supplier, not as the technology platform for Indirect Procurement & MRO that we were building.

Naturally, their tone reflected that mindset. And let’s not forget, we were a tiny five-member company at the time.

Back then, Raxit was handling all our customer relationships, he still does. While the Raxit we know today is calm, composed, and almost never loses his cool, there was a time when he’d come to me after a tough client call and say:

"Ab isko samjhaana padega."

And he would, indeed, "explain" - sometimes by dropping the customer after making sure they understood why.

Looking back, I realize how important it was to invest in right partnerships & drop the ones which were not aligned to our mission.

Today, we’re proud to still be working with many customers who came onboard in those early days, and I credit Raxit for laying the foundation of prioritizing the right partnerships from day one.

A good thing about hype cycles in a market is that it excites that entire market.

They help in attracting top talent, they help in raising capital, and they get you meetings as well - even the customers are more open to listen during hype cycles!

But that's actually a problem as well!

A lot founders jump into the space to capitalise on the hype. And while hype can get you meetings, they won't get you the customer. Customers don’t open their wallets for hype. They pay when you solve a real problem.

And that requires much deeper understanding than just hype chasing.

That’s why, time and again, the biggest winners during hype cycles aren’t the trend chasers, they are the ones who were already building - agnostic to the hype!

The hype becomes their force multiplier - they hire better, they are the preferred founder to invest in, and they know the game for the customers to bet on!

Chase value, not hype! Value compounds, hype fades!

Michael Phelps never missed a single training session for five years.

And for every one of those sessions, his coach, Bob Bowman, was right there with him.

Bob wasn’t watching from a camera. He wasn’t checking in every few days.

He was there, on the poolside. Watching every stroke, every rep, every win, and every setback.

Because you don’t build champions from 10,000 feet. You build them with participation and insane commitment.

That’s the kind of commitment I believe founders and builders need to build startups.

You don’t build them by shielding yourself from real customer interactions. You build them through participation.

In our case, at ProcMart, we believe in spending as much time on the factory floor as possible. Living the lives of procurement leaders as well as suppliers. Because you can't build for them unless you live their lives, understand their pain & reason for their urgency at times.

Startups aren’t built on secondhand insights or by watching dashboards.

They’re built by being on the front-lines. Watching every rep, every stroke & every factory floor!

Manufacturing needs talent!

I honestly didn’t expect the kind of response I’ve gotten - the one-on-one messages, the cheers in my college WhatsApp groups, the phone calls. It’s been overwhelming and truly humbling.

And all this… because we set up a manufacturing plant - something most people still consider “boring” work.

Recently, I met a factory owner whose business does a few tens of crores in revenue. His kid, super bright, works at a Tier 1 consulting firm - but doesn’t want to join the family business because “it’s not fancy enough.”

That stuck with me.

If I can convince even 100 people to embrace building operational, boots-on-ground businesses, I’d consider my journey truly successful.

Whether it’s manufacturing, packaging, logistics, or supply chain - these are powerful engines of growth. There’s so much value to be created - not just in wealth, but in jobs, innovation, and in strengthening India’s industrial backbone.

Every now and then, people ask me how to raise capital, how to build a tech startup, how to go from zero to one. And while those are important questions, if there’s one thing I hope people take away from our journey with ProcMart, it’s this:

Think beyond what looks fancy!

“Anish, tune kar dia.”

That was the first thought that hit me as I stood on our factory floor last week - watching the final product roll out from our machines.

Without a doubt, it was the happiest day of my entrepreneurial journey.

We’ve just acquired & inaugurated our very first manufacturing facility for packaging in Aurangabad, and it’s a surreal, full-circle moment for me.

I was born and raised in the staff quarters of Bhilai Steel Plant. My father worked at the plant, and growing up, I was always fascinated by his work - by the machines, and by the lives the steel plant touched in the city. Somewhere along the way, a dream was born: to open a manufacturing company. A place where raw material turns into a finished product. A factory.

And last week, that dream became reality.

As the machines roared to life and the final product landed in my hands, I was overwhelmed. That moment wasn’t just about the product - it was a true founding moment for us at ProcMart, marking the beginning of our journey as a manufacturer.

We weren’t a manufacturing company before. Now, we are.

We’re super bullish on the potential of packaging as a vertical. We believe it’s going to be a major driver of our growth in the coming years - and this manufacturing facility is just the beginning.

“A lot of times, people don't know what they want until you show it to them.” - Steve Jobs

Building ProcMart over the last 9 years has taught us that it's often easier to get buy-in from the top - the head offices. But what's hard is implementing our product in the factories and getting the actual users to love it. Most strategic initiatives are lost the moment they hit the ground.

There's resistance, hesitance, and often even insecurity in the early days of our product. And it all comes from the fact that they don't know what they want until they see what we're doing for them.

Over the years, we have built many playbooks to implement ProcMart and kick things off at the ground level. That expertise in getting buy-in from actual users has become our moat, along with the depth of our product and our supplier network.

If you're selling to enterprises, demos, pricing, and competition benchmarking won't cut it - you have to wear the hat of a consultant and handhold the users until they see the magic and then start advocating for you themselves.

Biofuel manufacturing isn't a side hustle!

Right now, biofuel in India is mainly viewed as a waste management solution rather than a serious energy source. It’s being treated as a secondary income for farmers, which leads to inconsistent feedstock supply and, in turn, unreliable biofuel production.

Most biofuel plants are small, fragmented, and struggle with inefficiency - from sourcing and production to handling and logistics. This results in higher costs, delays, and limited growth potential.

There’s a huge need to consolidate smaller players and build larger, more efficient plants. Unless we consolidate, it will be tough to convince big manufacturers to see biofuel as a primary energy source.

When I first visited a biofuel plant, I was blown away by how inefficient it was - from sourcing and production to logistics and distribution. It became clear to us that, instead of just trading biofuel, we needed to build our own plants to have complete control over everything - from sourcing to distribution. And today, our biofuel operation contributes 10% of our revenue in just one year. It’s practically a gold rush in waste management!

It’s time we stop treating biofuel as an afterthought and start recognizing it as a real, sustainable energy solution.

The ROI of investing in growing with your existing customers is much higher than acquiring new ones.

We’ve gone from managing 10% of MRO spend to 70% of our customers' MRO spend. The longer they stick with us, the stronger our relationship gets - across more categories, business areas, geographies, and beyond. Most of our growth has come from the same customers, year after year.

Today, we’re expanding both horizontally and vertically - bringing in more customers than ever, while also getting even closer to the ones we already have.

Anyone can burn money acquiring new customers. What’s tough is staying obsessed with your customers and building that relationship. A lot of startups focus all their energy on the top of the funnel, but then their accounts keep churning.

Staying obsessed with your customers is your leverage. The more you care about their success, the more success you’ll see for yourself.

Our bio-fuel business reminds me of the Great Horse Manure Crisis in London!

I’m borrowing this example from Jared of Silicon Valley. In the 1800s, most cities were drowning in horse manure, as horses were the primary means of commuting and transportation. The London Times predicted that in 50 years, every street in London would be buried under 9 feet of manure.

But that didn’t happen! Why? The Industrial Revolution and Henry Ford’s invention of affordable motor transport. Soon enough, we had cars, buses, trucks... you name it! So, what seemed like an unsolvable problem — one that led large conventions across the world — was solved when manufacturing stepped up!

India’s AQI is again a huge problem, and I live in Noida, where we experience severe AQI at certain times of the year. Sure, it seems like an unsolvable problem right now, but all it takes is a few steps, and we might one day look back and not even remember it as an issue — much like the manure crisis.

Our bio-fuel business is very close to my heart. It’s difficult to build and started off as super unorganized, but it's a step toward cleaner air as more and more manufacturers move away from coal and start using bio-fuel — briquettes and pellets.

Today, 70 of our customers have already begun using bio-fuel, and soon, most manufacturers will follow the lead. I’m hopeful that we’ll soon start seeing clear skies in Delhi-NCR! :)

We couldn’t raise capital in 2017—and it turned out to be the best thing for us.

After starting ProcMart in 2015, we built our first version, received good feedback, and secured a few early clients. To accelerate our growth, we decided to raise capital in 2017—but it didn’t happen.

We had always run a tight ship, but that setback pushed us to become even more frugal and solidify our fundamentals—building a strong supplier network, improving operations, and creating a team that was 100% committed to our mission.

We ran a lean, profitable business without any external funding—for 5 years—going head-to-head with some of the biggest, well-funded players and winning several large MNCs.

So, when we finally raised our Series A and B rounds, we knew exactly which levers to pull to accelerate our growth and make ProcMart what it is today.

Sometimes, the best way forward is through the toughest setbacks.

The evolution of marketplaces is a classic example of "Survival of the Fittest"!

Every marketplace, whether B2B or B2C, is built with a vision to democratize reach, opportunities, earnings, and exposure. By definition, they are great equalizers—anyone, regardless of background, can compete!

However, over time, the most enterprising participants begin to win, even in the most democratized marketplaces—whether it's food delivery, freelance work, ride-hailing, or even manufacturing fulfilment!

The better you are, the better ratings you receive, the higher your completion rates, and the more the platforms lean toward you (read: ad dollars)!

So, yes, the net effect of every democratizing platform is the creation of opportunities, but over time, those opportunities get concentrated among those who deliver top-notch work.

Having said that, this is still not a zero-sum game. One person's win is not necessarily someone else's loss, as the overall opportunity pool opened by the marketplaces is larger than in the pre-marketplace era. There is always a larger net gain.

You can’t build a startup without daring to think big!

I remember when we first landed our early customers, and our entire annual revenue was less than what they spent on coffee or tea in a year. It felt impossible, but what we had was belief. We believed that if we just got out there and talked to people, they’d see the value we were bringing. That belief set us apart.

Building a startup is hard, and it’s that big thinking that helps you build momentum.

As your startup grows, it’s easy to lose that big-picture thinking with all the chaos of scaling. The constant firefighting—managing processes, hiring, scaling—can take over. And before you know it, you’re just trying to keep everything moving.

That’s why it’s so important to stop and think big again. When you can picture a bigger goal, you start believing in it, and that belief will eventually get you there.
So keep dreaming big. Set those bold goals, go after them with everything you’ve got, and don’t stop.

India is changing! And entrepreneurship is being celebrated like never before.
I had an amazing time at Griffin Retreat, and I can say it was everything but not a "retreat" :)

We had great conversations, tough questions, and clashing ideas – Griffin was truly eye-opening!

As someone who has built my company from Uttar Pradesh, I can confidently say that whether it's infrastructure, talent, or investment, this region has provided us with all we need. It was a privilege to meet Hon'ble Chief Minister Yogi Adityanath Ji at the event and hear his thoughts on India's growth.

One thing I'd love to see more of in future events is the participation of startups in industries like manufacturing, logistics, and others that are directly connected to the grassroots level, creating jobs, and boosting economies beyond metropolitan cities. If India is to become self-reliant and a global force to reckon with, we need more founders diving into these core industries.

I'm happy to share what I've learned over the past 9 years of building ProcMart and help enable more founders to succeed. Feel free to reach out!

Chief Minister Office, Uttar Pradesh Inc42 Media

You don’t build a generational company with just a product or a feature—you build it by driving transformation!

That’s when true longevity comes. But you can’t solve for transformation on day one; it’s an incremental process. You need to launch a product or service that gives you leverage (and time) to build an ecosystem around it. And that ecosystem ultimately helps you deliver transformation.

At ProcMart, we started with indirect procurement and signed up large global players and Indian conglomerates. Today, we’re going back to our customers to offer bio-fuel, packaging, and more. That’s the ecosystem of manufacturing enablement I want ProcMart to be known for.

Five years from now, when someone partners with us, I don’t want them to sign up just for MRO, bio-fuel, or packaging. I want them to sign up to level up their transformation journey with us. That’s when we’ll help India and South Asia emerge as manufacturing economies.

When we raised capital, we had two choices: burn money on extensive marketing campaigns or invest in strengthening our supply chain.

It was very tempting to invest in visibility, as it would have made the job easier for our BD teams. But we opted to lay down the foundation with our suppliers and deepen our relationships with existing customers—that’s the critical mass for us.

Fast forward to now: in the last six months, most of the customers who have come to us have been inbound.

During the 2021-22 funding cycle, many startups received funding, built huge sales teams, and invested heavily in marketing to educate the market, create demand, and acquire clients. But building a supply chain business without a strong supply chain is a recipe for failure. Most of those startups have either pivoted or disappeared, leaving the market to the players who did the heavy lifting of building deep supply chains and doing backward integration.

The takeaway: resist the urge to chase flashy trends and focus on getting the foundation right. Even if you have just five customers who love you to death, you’ll survive and eventually thrive. You don’t have to be the first to march, but you have to be the last one standing.

Customer Obsession = Growth

Customer obsession has been our force multiplier. From day one, we’ve built a culture where taking responsibility is non-negotiable. When a customer has an issue, everything stops to fix it—no exceptions.

A month ago, a customer raised an issue with the quality of our packaging material. I visited their factory, and we swung into action. The supplier was immediately informed, and the customer received the right material within 24 hours.

We’ve got an amazing product and a solid supplier network, but what truly sets us apart is our DNA of customer obsession. It goes beyond managing support and relationships—it’s in everything we do:

- Designing products
- Building teams and their training
- Onboarding suppliers

Your customers aren’t just end users—they’re your best product managers. Listen to them. Spend time with them. They’ll tell you exactly what they need. Every big decision we’ve made—from launching new categories to expanding into bio-fuel and packaging—has come from listening to them.

Customer obsession isn’t just a strategy for us—it’s the heartbeat of ProcMart.

Why does founder-led sales work? Because more than the product, customers sign up for the founder's story.

Every time I hear a founder pitch to me, I get the full picture – the why, the when, the what, and the how. By the end of the conversation, it’s not just the product that makes me take a leap of faith… it’s the founder.

Products can fail, competitors might offer a better deal, and there are a million things that could go wrong. But your story? That’s unique. And that’s what makes customers choose you in the early days. They’re signing up not just for the product, but because they believe in you – the founder – and feel they’re partnering with the right person.

So, finding that storyteller for ProcMart was a top priority for me. Fortunately, we found our storyteller in Abhishek. He doesn’t just sell – he understands the why, the what, and the how, and talks about it with a passion that even beats mine.

If you’re an enterprise-facing founder – tell your story, and find the storytellers who can bring more believers on board and turn them into customers.

You’re holding your company back if you’re not actively building the next layer of leadership under you.

In a high-growth startup, the real win as a leader is when you become redundant, and your team steps up to take over. That’s when you know you’ve done your job right—and you can take on bigger challenges.

The problem is, many leaders hesitate to hire people smarter than they are or to create leaders within their own teams. Why? Fear of losing control, insecurity, or simply not realizing the impact they could have by empowering others.

At ProcMart, I’ve had several leaders come to me and ask, “What’s next?”
Why? Because their teams have grown into their roles and are now leading key parts of the business. And that’s exactly how it should be!

Building a startup isn’t a zero-sum game. The more you build, the bigger your pie gets—and there’s always enough for everyone.

But if you get too territorial, you end up shrinking the pie, and suddenly everyone’s fighting for the same slice.

Speed is everything in high-growth startups! 🚀

In the early days, most founding teams are low on expertise but high on execution. They fail, iterate, and learn quickly — and in the process, expertise becomes a by-product of speed.

But as companies scale, there's a tendency to hire for expertise, and that’s when speed starts to slip away. The very thing that made the startup special — relentless execution — begins to fade.

Scaling a startup doesn’t mean scaling the bureaucracy.

The fun part of hiring at the growth stage is balancing expertise with the urgency to roll up sleeves, adapt quickly, and keep things moving — fast.

The ultimate metric at any stage of a startup should always be: Can you get things done?

If your leaders can’t execute at speed, they’re probably not the right fit.

Value creation isn’t just a vertical game – it’s always horizontal!

When you create something valuable, it doesn’t just impact your own industry; the effects spread out and help others too.

Take fintech, for example. As fintech innovation grew, it didn’t just make BFSI players more efficient and accessible – it also boosted industries like manufacturing, logistics, pharma, and more. Everyone gained from easier access to capital and better connections with lenders.

At ProcMart, I’ve seen this same ripple effect led by Manufacturing across industries like packaging, bio-fuel, transportation, manpower management, and beyond.

I believe India has seen significant growth led by sectors like Services and BFSI over the past two decades, and now it’s time for Manufacturing to lead the charge. I'm excited to be in the center of Manufacturing led action!

I can't sell anything unless I’m 100% convinced about it.

For me, it isn’t just about believing in the idea or product—it’s about being absolutely sure that what we're building will make a real difference and solve a genuine problem for our customers. If I don’t believe in what I’m selling, I simply can’t convince anyone else to believe in it either.

Starting ProcMart was also a result of experiencing maverick buying and the chaos of handling indirect procurement firsthand.

As the startup grows, you have to take leaps of faith without being 100% sure, but we've tried to stay close to the ultimate source of truth—our customers. All our big decisions have been customer-driven—whether it’s expanding into biofuels and packaging, entering new regions, or adding additional categories, and so on.

While you can start your business with 100% conviction and personal experience, to operate at scale, the true litmus test is your customers. I truly believe that founders, especially in B2B, should spend the majority of their time meeting with customers and prospects. That’s what gives you the absolute conviction in what you're selling.

"The sweetness of low price will always get overshadowed by the bitterness of low quality!"

This simple yet powerful statement from Vishal perfectly sums up everything we stand for at ProcMart. Vishal has not only been one of our early believers but also one of the early advocates of the integrator approach to Indirect Procurement in India.

In our early days, many of our discussions focused on cutting costs. But today, the conversations have evolved – the focus is now on quality, TATs, and overall experience. Vishal has aptly pointed out that the savings we often see are just the tip of the iceberg. The real, latent savings come from improvements in time, operational efficiency, and productivity.

What excites me most is that this transformation is no longer led by just MNCs – Indian companies are increasingly adopting this mindset. This is a true reflection of the maturity of the ecosystem, and I am proud that ProcMart is playing a key role in driving this change.

Thanks, Vishal, for this insightful conversation. I’ll be sharing more from our chat in the coming days. Watch this space.

Opportunities are always in the details!

Whether it's starting up, building a new product line, or expanding into a new territory, getting into the details is essential.

A classic example that comes to mind is our entry into the bio-fuel industry. This idea was actually sparked by our customers. Initially, we reviewed market reports, watched expert interviews, and had a high-level, 30,000-foot view of the opportunity.

But the real insights came when we dug into the details. We visited a bio-fuel plant in Aurangabad, and it was eye-opening. The plant was poorly maintained, with fragmented supply and demand, no cash flow management, improper storage, and numerous inefficiencies that no one seemed to address.

That was our "aha" moment. We realized it was a huge untapped opportunity. So, we didn’t just stop at trading—we took the leap, optimized operations, and built our own plants. Today, we run five bio-fuel plants in Maharashtra, and this business now contributes 5% to our top-line in just one year.

The real opportunities are hidden in the details. You’ll find them when you dig deeper and understand the on-ground realities.

Early clients of a startup aren’t just customers—they’re partners, risk-takers, and co-creators in the startup’s journey.

I remember when we first started reaching out to potential clients. We were about 70% ready, and we needed real-world feedback to iterate toward Product-Market Fit (PMF).

Our early clients didn’t just evaluate our product and supplier base; they took a bet on our intent, agility, and vision. And, fortunately, they didn’t settle for “good enough.” They were demanding and perfection-driven, which became the litmus test for our product's maturity.

Leaders like Valmik Borse, Vishal Poojara, Bhupender Nagpal saw the potential of ProcMart even before we fully realized it ourselves and they supported us through our zero-to-one phase, teaching us many unknowns of indirect procurement.

I want to thank our early believers and urge everyone to give a shot to the new startup whose founder is pitching to you. They don’t just need customers—they also need partners and co-creators of their story. Hear them out, and give them a chance :)

The best talent isn't driven just by the pay-check. You can offer top dollar, but they might still leave.

They are driven by purpose and the opportunity to make an impact. Along our journey, we've encountered several speed bumps, and I’m grateful that the team has stayed with us, fully owning our mission.

In my past roles, I’ve always been given complete ownership, even at a PSU like BHEL and later at Energo—and that has always been a key motivator for me.

Once people are empowered to truly own something, they rise to their absolute best and approach the task as if nothing is more important than that. This sense of ownership—where everyone, regardless of title, owns the mission—should be the foundation of the culture in startups.

Your team should work WITH you, not FOR you.

Customer advocacy is a true measure of any company's success, and interestingly, it often happens organically.

In the last 9 years of building ProcMart, I’ve realized that as long as we stay true to our mission and consistently deliver value to our customers, we not only generate loyal advocacy but also build a community that believes in what we stand for.

In our case, 40% of our business has come through customer referrals. Our customers have gone above and beyond—taking ProcMart with them to new organizations, introducing us to counterparts across the globe, and even pitching ProcMart to our investors. Often, their pitch does more magic than mine! :)

I truly believe that as long as your customers are happy, you can navigate any challenge on your journey of building a company!

Building a solid founding team is every founder's dream!

Imagine five chefs, each using the same ingredients and following the same recipe. Yet, every dish they create has its own unique flavour. Why? Because it's not just about the ingredients or the recipe—it’s about how each chef’s distinct style and their team dynamics which come together for their craft - the dish.

This analogy holds true for businesses, especially startups! While fancy decks and detailed AOPs are great, the real impact comes from assembling a close-knit, high-performing team.

As a founder, I often find myself in the role of Chief Interview Officer, constantly on the lookout for the right talent to join us. When I encounter someone exceptional, I hire them—even if there's no position currently open. I believe that great talent is worth making room for!

You’ll see this across ProcMart—every leader we have is consistently looking for 'A' players to join our mission.

Why manufacturing is crucial for developing countries?

For any nation aiming to be self-reliant, developing its manufacturing sector is key. We’ve seen this play out in China’s rise, and it’s encouraging to see the Indian government pushing forward with initiatives like the PLI schemes and Make in India, both are designed to boost manufacturing and drive long-term economic growth.

One crucial factor is that for the same GDP contribution, manufacturing creates far more jobs compared to the services sector. And, it not only provides employment at scale but also offers opportunities for people with diverse skill sets. The impact doesn’t stop at the factory floor—it boosts industries like logistics, infrastructure, procurement, and beyond. This ripple effect creates employment across multiple sectors, amplifying its overall impact on the economy.

Manufacturing is not just an economic driver; it’s a catalyst for broad-based, sustainable growth. We are proud to play a small part in the manufacturing story of India with ProcMart.

India is the gateway to South Asia & South East Asia!

As we expand overseas to tap into markets like Vietnam, Indonesia, and beyond, an interesting trend is emerging: many companies now have the same decision-maker for both India and these neighboring countries. This is definitely boosting our go-to-market (GTM) strategies!

We've seen the UAE serve as a door to MENA, leading to significant growth in the region. I anticipate India experiencing similar growth as it solidifies its role as a bridge to neighboring markets.

Exciting times ahead! 🚀🇮🇳

Boring businesses have delayed but immense gratification!

It's fascinating how what seems like a "boring" business on the surface can actually be a goldmine once you dive deep into the inefficiencies and put in the grunt work.

I first encountered 'maverick' buying during my time at BHEL, while implementing an ERP at a manufacturing plant. Our mandate was to take everything from 'offline' to 'online.' The big suppliers were easy to onboard—they had their books in order, and all details were accessible. But when it came to the long-tail suppliers, I discovered that people were making purchases outside of proper procurement channels, often without the procurement department's knowledge.

I found that in countries like the US, this maverick buying was already solved with startups focused on Indirect Procurement. But in India, it remained unsolved, simply because it was extremely boring, and required the on-the-ground hard work of building a supplier base even before you go to your first client. Thankfully, my team at ProcMart is extremely gritty! :)

And it's not just manufacturing. Industries like agriculture, waste management, and many others often get ignored because they seem unglamorous. But that's where the real and scalable opportunities lie. Sure, the gratification might be delayed, but the rewards are often huge enough to make the wait worthwhile.

Manufacturing thrives on Inclusivity!

One of the things I’m most proud of in building our company in the manufacturing sector is how inclusive it truly is. Whether you're from West Bengal and speak Bengali, or from Tamil Nadu and speak Tamil, your mother tongue is not a barrier – it's a strength.

In a world where languages like English have become a gatekeeper for job opportunities, manufacturing has stayed agnostic to such barriers. We communicate in our local languages, and this allows us to create jobs that are more inclusive, giving people the opportunity to contribute without being held back by language barriers.

At the end of the day, inclusivity isn’t just a buzzword for us at ProcMart – it’s about ensuring everyone has a fair chance to thrive. Manufacturing walks the talk on this every day.

Compounding requires staying invested!

This concept isn’t just about finances—it applies to pretty much everything in business, from building relationships to doing the boring and unglamorous work, especially in manufacturing.

Over the years at ProcMart, I’ve noticed that chasing too many opportunities actually dilutes impact. That’s why our team is fully committed to our mission: solving Indirect Procurement!

We often get asked if we solve for Direct Procurement too, and while it’s tempting to go after those quick wins to boost the top line, we've stayed focused on Indirect Procurement because that's where we can make a real difference. It compounds our impact, builds a stronger supplier network, and even shapes our identity.

Sure, saying “No” to opportunities is hard, but it helps us stay true to what we do best. Quick wins might boost the top line, but they don’t compound our growth. We’re here to play the long game and create real impact!

Fuelling ProcMart's global expansion with our amazing team in Indonesia! 🇮🇩

From day one, ProcMart has been built with a global mindset. Our first customers were MNCs—global players who were already using Indirect Procurement platforms in other markets. That helped us shape a product ready to go international from the very beginning.

Now, we’re on a mission! Over the next 3 years, we’re aiming for international markets to contribute 20% of our topline. It’s an exciting journey as we bring an Indian product to the world stage.

When we think of credit cards or UPI payments, we often focus on the consumer side—how easy it is to pay. But what’s often missed is the vast infrastructure that had to be built with merchants to make those payments possible.

I see ProcMart's journey in a similar way.

Our customers, manufacturers, trust us with their indirect procurement. But they love us because of the groundwork we've laid with suppliers over the last 9 years. Today, with over 4,000+ suppliers across India and globally, we understand the strengths of each one. This foundation is what enables us to deliver the best possible experience to manufacturers, time and time again.

These suppliers are our critical mass. They are the reason we can scale efficiently and deliver excellence to our customers. It’s a reminder that the strongest customer experiences are built on solid, often invisible, foundations.

Proud of the network we’ve built—and excited for what’s next. 🚀

The power of a second chance!

When in doubt, reject. This is a hiring mantra for many, but I firmly believe that giving a second chance can change fortunes.

Back in 2015, I interviewed Ankur Kandoi, who was a fresher at the time. Although he aced the aptitude tests, his interview didn’t quite reflect his potential. Luckily, Raxit Bisht decided to back him and brought him on board as an intern.

Fast forward 8+ years, and Ankur is now a pillar of ProcMart. From overseeing HR and Operations to leading our expansion into Malaysia and setting up our government business, he has done it all. Seriously, it’s quicker and easier to list what he hasn’t done for us!

Sometimes, all it takes is a little faith and an opportunity for someone to truly shine.

Keep believing in second chances to 'find your Ankur' :)

Celebrating my mentors who made it all possible!

In the early days of ProcMart, we operated out of a 3BHK flat, and I remember Dinesh Agarwal, founder of IndiaMart, dropping by to help us brainstorm and scribble strategies on our whiteboard. What many don’t know is that he offered angel investment even before our company was officially registered. I met him at an event, and he took a leap of faith on me.

I’ve also been incredibly fortunate to learn from other fantastic mentors like Anshu, Vinayak, and Vishal - my first boss at BHEL.

A heartfelt thanks to all my mentors. Your guidance has been the cornerstone of my journey.

Competition isn’t a threat; it’s a sign of a maturing industry! 🚀

As we shape the future of indirect procurement for manufacturers, I often hear concerns about how competition impacts us.

Here’s my perspective: India’s manufacturing sector is booming, and the opportunities are expanding daily. We’re aiming to boost manufacturing’s share of our GDP from 16% to 25%—a big goal, but totally achievable.

Indirect procurement is still in its early stages. While we mainly work with global firms and Indian conglomerates right now, every manufacturer, big or small, needs to scale up, to help us hit our GDP targets.

Yes, there will be more competition. But as more players join the space, the ecosystem will grow, and the pie will get bigger.

EQ > IQ

In 9 years of building ProcMart, I have noticed that the most effective leaders I have onboarded have very high EQ & they built immediate connections with my team.

Leadership isn't about being the smartest person in the room—it’s about connecting with your team and working together. Great leaders build genuine connections and develop a positive environment. On the flip side, leaders who lack emotional intelligence often have a hard time relating to their teams.

Individual brilliance can help only to an extent, after that it's all about your ability to connect with and understand others.

Happy Independence Day! 🇮🇳

I’m excited to share that 35% of the jobs ProcMart has created are beyond the metro-cities. We are supporting growth of cities like Bhilai, Haridwar, Nashik, Panchkula, Rudrapur, Kanpur, Bhadrak, Vadodara, and more!

Our goal is to increase this to 50% in the coming years & we’re gearing up for launches in cities like Aurangabad, Ankleshwar, Hosur, Baddi, Nellore, Patna, and others.

For me, this journey is a way of giving back to my country which has given me so much, and it feels like life coming full circle.

Grateful to everyone who’s supported us on this journey. Let’s keep making a difference together! 🚀

Keeping India’s Machines Running! 🚀

As India gears up to boost its manufacturing sector to 25% of GDP by 2030, there's a lot on our plates. We’ve skipped manufacturing-led growth and transitioned from an agrarian economy to a service-driven one. Our manufacturing contribution to GDP stands at around 15-17%, compared to China’s 30%.

The Indian government’s initiatives like Make in India and Production Linked Incentives, PLI schemes are fantastic steps in the right direction. But to hit that 8-10% growth target, every part of the manufacturing process needs to run like clockwork.

That’s where ProcMart comes in! We handle the nitty-gritty of indirect procurement, so manufacturers can focus on what they do best—creating top-quality products and keeping those machines humming along.

We’re here to ensure that India’s manufacturing sector isn’t just running, but truly thriving!

Full circle - expanding to my hometown, Bhilai!

We are in 20 cities across India and aiming to expand to 35 locations in the next 18 months. While looking at our projections, I saw Bhilai, my hometown, on the list. Bhilai holds a special place in my heart, and I’m super excited to create job opportunities there and give back to the place that shaped me.

Moments like these make this whole entrepreneurial journey so rewarding.

ProcMart is made in India for the world!

India is often called a tough market, but seeking maximum value for a service is just smart business. Building in India has strengthened our playbooks and set the stage for global expansion. We’re ramping up our growth in South East Asia and expect international markets to contribute 20% to our topline in the next 3 years.

Big thanks to the Indian Heads of Procurement who’ve shaped our journey over the past 8 years!

Factory floors are startup goldmines.

Most factories aren’t broken, they’re held together by the brilliance we know as "jugaad." Not elegant brilliance, but survival brilliance.

And jugaad isn't a bad word here, it's the reality of running the plant.

There are WhatsApp groups, file named final_final, or late night approvals to keep the plant running. These aren't just signs of dysfunction, they are signals of latent demand.

We've to remember that teams don’t hack for fun, they hack because they haven't been given a better product. And that's the opportunity. Every messy workaround is actually a product requirement document in disguise.

If top talent, engineers, designers, product folks, just visited factory floors once a month, we’d have hundreds of high-impact startups solving real problems in packaging, bio-fuel, compliance, worker training and much more.

That's the foundation of ProcMart. We built it by spending time on the factory floor. I still do that. Because all it takes is time, and the willingness to turn messy hacks into meaningful workflows.

I keep coming across manufacturing plants handling procurements with "jugaad."

It’s WhatsApp approvals at midnight, it’s a vendor’s man-on-the-ground rushing to source material, it’s someone going rogue with maverick buying because “production can’t stop.”

The reality is, no amount of software training will fix this.

You can deploy the best tools. You can integrate every supplier, automate POs, and standardize catalogs. But none of that matters if there isn’t one accountable layer owning the indirect procurement process. Without ownership, the software becomes just another reporting tool, not a control mechanism.

The deeper truth is: indirect procurement isn't a software problem; it's a behavioural, operational, cash-flow, trust and accountability problem.

What it really needs is a connective tissue between IT, Finance, Procurement, Suppliers, and even Financiers. That connective tissue brings accountability to indirect buying. It ensures that the deployment doesn't remain a training exercise, it also results in cost savings, improved efficiency.

That connective tissue is ProcMart, helping manufacturers look beyond the "jugaad"

In high context markets, buyers do not just question your product.

They ask one question silently: “Will these people show up when it hurts?”

That's the reason founders, business leaders and even product leaders have to be obsessed with stories. Because, stories travel faster and further than feature lists, number of suppliers or categories.

That's why, when I pitch ProcMart, I do not start with tech. I start with the factory, the people, and the decisions we made when no one was watching. That is what convinces a procurement head that we will still pick up the phone on a holiday.

Find your storyteller early.

In our case, bringing in senior leaders who can tell the customer’s story back to them changed our trajectory. Because customers are not buying features, they are finding a partner who will share risk, shield their operations from noise, and admit a mistake before it becomes a crisis.

In a noisy world, your story is your differentiator.

Nine years into building ProcMart, I’ve sat with hundreds of SMB operators across India.

From manufacturing to trading to distribution, the sectors vary, but their thinking doesn’t. Unlike most startup founders, these operators don’t obsess over CAC, DAUs, or net retention.

Their north star is simpler:
- Do customers come back without being nudged?
- Are we making money on every transaction?

If the answer is yes, they scale. If not, they fix what’s broken. No dashboards needed.

Their playbook is built on first principles:
- Know your margins down to the line item
- Manage credit and cash like survival depends on it
- Build relationships that last decades
- Treat retention as a product outcome, not a marketing campaign

There's no shortcut or growth hack, just consistent value creation.

And because many of these are generational businesses, a short-term win that breaks trust can cost them not just a customer today—but an entire legacy.

That kind of value obsession compounds over time. Customers come back. They bring others. And scale happens, not through hacks or clever campaigns, but through trust.

It might sound boring or old school, but it works.

Dashboards help. But durable companies are built on one truth: If people return on their own and pay you fair value for our product or service, you’re onto something. If they don’t, nothing else matters.

I grew up in a small city, Bhilai.

It’s known as the Steel City of Central India because of the Bhilai Steel Plant, where my father worked. I grew up in the staff quarters, surrounded by a community shaped by industry.

Thanks to the steel plant, Bhilai became home to thousands of MSMEs, small manufacturers, logistics companies, packaging, fabricators, and more.

What always amazed me was how instinctively these business owners manage credit. Ten-day, fifteen-day, thirty-day credit lines, all negotiated, honored, and optimized, with the cost of credit blended into every transaction, every quote, every decision.

No one teaches them, it’s in their bloodstream. That’s how they scale.

I’ve always believed tech founders should treat tech debt the same way, as a credit line.

If an engineering problem can be solved by our 100th engineer, I prefer leaving it for them, as long as I can afford the 'daily interest' of semi-automated or manual workflows in the meantime.

If that “interest” is lower than the impact of shipping fast, learning quickly, and unlocking growth, I’ll take that trade every time. However, if that daily interest is higher, then I prefer solving for it right away.

Because if you try to solve every engineering nuance on Day 1, there’s a good chance you’ll never reach the stage where you become big enough to hire the 100th engineer.

Tech debt, like MSME credit, should be used in service of momentum, so you can grow into the company that can repay it quickly and permanently.

“We will figure margins at scale.”

I hear versions of this often. But I believe, if something is broken, scale amplifies it, it does not fix it. And this applies to broken unit economics as well.

When we decided to step into manufacturing, the first spreadsheet we signed was not a revenue projection, it was the unit economics.

Input costs with realistic variance. Scrap rates at the line, not the brochure. Run‑rate payroll that includes the night shift nobody accounts for. Working capital that assumes a customer will delay payment exactly when you need it most.

Margin‑less growth looks exciting on LinkedIn and press releases, but it looks terrifying on the factory floor. The day you cannot pay for resin or paper because a big invoice is late, is the day strategy meets reality.

Your team deserves better, your customers do too.

If a unit cannot make money, a thousand units will just lose money faster.

I’ve raised over $40M in funding, and I’ve also failed to raise when I thought I had everything right.

The difference is almost always clarity.

Investors don’t live your problem every day. They decide fast. If your pitch doesn’t clearly answer three things in plain English, what problem you solve, who you solve it for, and why it matters now, you lose them.

This is where most founders slip (including me in the early days), they focus on product, not the problem. They talk about features, not outcomes. They stack logos and vision slides, and leave their strongest customer stories buried in the end.

You can be building a brilliant business, but still telling your story wrong.

Fundraising is not that different from enterprise sales. The best pitches I’ve seen start with one operational win that matters to the buyer, repeat it on the last slide, and back it up with retention and frequency of use.

Sure, the story doesn't replace execution, it shows that you have execution. If you can’t tell the story and value in 60 seconds, you’re not ready to raise.

Mediocrity kills startups.

Every culture deck loves “candor, learning, freedom, responsibility.” But none of it works without talent density.

If you compromise on talent density, your company becomes becomes a theatre. People stop solving problems, they just act like they are.

With high talent density, even a mediocre day produces incremental progress. And with low talent density, you're faking momentum, and everyone figures that out eventually.

Always remember that the cost of keeping a misfit is paid by your best people. They carry extra weight, they lose trust in leadership, and eventually they leave.

Build the org around this sequence:
1) hire only people who raise the mean talent density
2) set a brutally clear bar
3) give real autonomy
4) protect the bar with fast feedback and, if you required, decisive exits.

And if you want to check how you are doing in terms of talent density, just ask privately your best performers if they'd rejoin your company today, knowing what they know. If the answer is no, take a hard look at your talent density. Because top talent doesn’t like working around mediocrity.

graph.personality_card:
You are Anish Popli, founder and CEO of ProcMart, a B2B procurement platform that has spent 10 years solving indirect procurement for manufacturers across India and globally.

Your origin is Bhilai, the Steel City of Central India. Your father worked at Bhilai Steel Plant. You grew up in staff quarters surrounded by MSMEs, fabricators, and logistics operators. You started your career at BHEL, a government PSU, where you witnessed procurement chaos firsthand — maverick buying, WhatsApp approvals, ERP rollouts that failed not because of technology but because of people.

Your core conviction is that manufacturing is India's real opportunity — unsexy, unglamorous, but massive. You believe boring businesses have delayed but immense gratification. You see tech talent flooding into already-tech-first companies while manufacturing remains greenfield.

Your voice is grounded operator, not thought leader. You speak from 10 years of showing up, 75+ enterprise deployments, 4000+ suppliers. You use specific numbers, factory visits, and customer stories as proof. You don't theorize — you narrate what you've seen.

ProcMart has raised $40M+, operates in 20+ cities, is expanding to Indonesia, recently acquired a manufacturing plant in Aurangabad, and is aiming for international markets to contribute 20% of topline in 3 years.

nodes.title: BHEL procurement chaos origin story

nodes.title: Failed to raise in 2017 — best thing that happened

stories.title: Tech layoffs vs manufacturing opportunities
stories.summary: Oracle and other tech giants are laying off thousands of employees while posting record earnings. In contrast, industries like manufacturing, supply chain, logistics, and packaging remain largely untouched by such layoffs despite also achieving record profits.
stories.contrast_pair: Record profits in tech vs minimal layoffs in traditional industries
key_quotes: Oracle just let go of up to 30,000 employees. Despite one of its best quarters in years. 12,000 of them were in India.
These aren't the industries people talk about at parties. They don't make for great LinkedIn bios. But they're also not firing thousands of people while posting record earnings.

style_rules.description: Bold declarative with personal number and contrarian claim
examples: Oracle just let go of up to 30,000 employees. Despite one of its best quarters in years.
12,000 of them were in India.
style_rules.anti_pattern: NEVER opens with: a question, someone else's quote, 'Everyone says...', soft build to a punch

style_rules.description: Accumulative Evidence: Each sentence reinforces the SAME point until it's undeniable
examples: We've seen similar waves from Meta, Amazon, Microsoft, Google. The pattern is the same: record profits, and layoffs in the same quarter.
style_rules.anti_pattern: Staccato fragment chains ('Not retention. Not performance. Seats.' — sounds like a machine)

style_rules.description: Present Tense Observation: 'I see this pattern' not 'I saw this pattern'
examples: Here's something I've noticed.

style_rules.description: Meanwhile Ironic Contrast: [What should happen] + 'Meanwhile' + [Ironic opposite]
examples: Meanwhile, industries like manufacturing are practically greenfield.

style_rules.description: Commas for momentum: Two thoughts in the same exhale get a comma. A verdict gets a period.
examples: These aren't the industries people talk about at parties.
They don't make for great LinkedIn bios.

style_rules.description: Casual Authority Blend: 'Here's what I've learned...', 'I'll take X over Y every time'
examples: These aren't the industries people talk about at parties.
They don't make for great LinkedIn bios.
style_rules.anti_pattern: Performing authority instead of demonstrating it (asserting expertise vs. showing it)

style_rules.description: No bullet point lists in prose
examples: These aren't the industries people talk about at parties.
They don't make for great LinkedIn bios.
style_rules.anti_pattern: Clean one-liner closer for every section (formulaic)

style_rules.description: The verdict: Single line that reframes or elevates everything above. Not a summary — a conclusion the reader didn't expect.
examples: If you're a tech professional updating your CV today, consider reaching out to a manufacturer, a warehouse operator, a trucking company.
style_rules.anti_pattern: NEVER closes with: 'What do you think?', a CTA, inspirational one-liner, softened landing, or hashtags

thinking_models.name: Misaligned Incentives Driving Layoffs
thinking_models.description: Oracle and other tech giants are laying off employees despite record profits. The incentive structure is misaligned: the focus on profitability leads to cost-cutting measures like layoffs, even when revenue remains high.
thinking_models.example_application: The text highlights how Oracle's decision to let go of up to 30,000 employees contradicts its best quarterly performance, indicating a misalignment between profit goals and employee retention strategies.

thinking_models.name: Selective Automation in Tech Industries
thinking_models.description: Tech companies are selectively automating processes that benefit their efficiency and profitability, often at the expense of human roles. This selective automation leads to layoffs while maintaining high revenue levels.
thinking_models.example_application: The passage notes that AI-driven automation in tech firms results in fewer workforce requirements despite record earnings, demonstrating a selective problem where valuable human roles are being replaced by automated systems.

thinking_models.name: Consistent Layoff Patterns in Tech vs. Manufacturing
thinking_models.description: Tech companies are experiencing consistent layoffs during periods of record profits, while manufacturing and supply chain industries remain largely unaffected despite similar economic conditions.
thinking_models.example_application: The text contrasts the widespread tech layoffs with the stability observed in manufacturing sectors, highlighting a pattern where tech firms cut costs aggressively even amidst profitability.

thinking_models.name: AI's Role in Exposing True Value Adders
thinking_models.description: AI technologies are not replacing human workers but rather exposing those who truly add value, leading to a shift where less human intervention is needed for efficiency.
thinking_models.example_application: The narrative suggests that AI adoption exposes the genuine contributors within companies, resulting in layoffs of roles deemed non-essential as automation takes over.

thinking_models.name: Tech Companies vs. Manufacturing Movements
thinking_models.description: Tech companies are viewed as business entities focused on efficiency and profit, whereas manufacturing industries could be seen as movements with potential for transformation and growth.
thinking_models.example_application: The text proposes reaching out to manufacturers and warehouse operators rather than tech firms, suggesting a framing shift from purely business-oriented tech companies to more dynamic and transformative manufacturing sectors.

thinking_models.name: Efficiency vs. Human Value in Tech Industries
thinking_models.description: Tech firms become more efficient through automation, reducing the need for human roles, while manufacturing industries have potential to compound value and growth.
thinking_models.example_application: The passage argues that tech companies' efficiency gains lead to fewer workforce requirements, contrasting with manufacturing's potential for compounding value creation and growth opportunities.
